locations.name: Bald Mountain Lode
locations.description: Backed by modest Eastern capital and big local ambition, the Bald Mountain Lode sank its first shaft in 1891 and quickly proved that the quartz veins of this district ran deeper and richer than surface sampling had suggested. The footwall side of the vein consistently ran higher in gold than the hanging wall, and experienced miners learned to work the ore selectively to maximize recovery and minimize dilution. Company housing lined a terrace above the mill; the rows of identical frame houses, company store, and assay office gave the camp the look of a planned industrial town. The head frame was a landmark for miles around, its silhouette visible from the valley road and serving as a symbol of the camp's industrial ambition. A union organizer arrived in the spring of the mine's busiest year and spent months carefully building a membership base before calling the strike that ultimately improved wage rates across the whole district. The mine's ore reserve was carefully blocked out level by level; the systematic approach — unusual for its era — allowed management to plan production and investment on a multi-year horizon. Employment peaked at around eighty men during the best years, with a ten-stamp mill and a complete surface plant that included a machine shop and carpenter shed. Years of weathering and vandalism have reduced the surface structures to their essence, but the mine remains a recognizable industrial landscape rather than just a scar on the hillside. This Black Hills location spans a patchwork of jurisdictions — contact the relevant land management office before bringing a metal detector, as regulations differ significantly by parcel.
locations.notes: Terry Peak district, Lawrence County, SD

locations.name: Terry Peak Gold Lode
locations.description: When oxidized silver minerals were found beneath a gossan cap in 1902, the Terry Peak Gold Lode was organized and sinking was begun before the summer was out. Chloride silver ore predominated in the upper oxidized zone, giving spectacular assay values that attracted early investment, but the primary sulfide ore below required a more sophisticated milling approach. The company's annual reports to shareholders — several of which survive in state archives — provide unusually detailed production and cost data for a mine of this size and era. Powder men carefully drilled and loaded shot holes at the end of each shift, timing blasts for the change-over period when the workings were clear of men. The nearby town that developed to service the mine's workforce was a rough-edged but functional community, its main street lined with frame buildings that housed every service a working miner's paycheck could buy. The company maintained an on-site blacksmith who sharpened drill steel daily, a vital operation in an era when hand-steel consumed more than it consumed drill bits. Employment peaked at around eighty men during the best years, with a ten-stamp mill and a complete surface plant that included a machine shop and carpenter shed. The old camp site and mine workings have slowly been absorbed into the surrounding landscape, but careful observers can still find the collapsed ruins of the ore bins and the distinctive gray-white tailings. This Black Hills location spans a patchwork of jurisdictions — contact the relevant land management office before bringing a metal detector, as regulations differ significantly by parcel.
locations.notes: Terry Peak district, Lawrence County, SD

locations.name: Terry Peak Silver Lode
locations.description: Silver-lead float scattered across the hillside in 1894 told experienced prospectors exactly where to sink the first shaft of the Terry Peak Silver Lode. The complex mineralogy — argentite, polybasite, and stephanite alongside common galena — required a selective milling approach that the company's metallurgist took months to perfect. Powder men carefully drilled and loaded shot holes at the end of each shift, timing blasts for the change-over period when the workings were clear of men. The shaft was sunk in stages, each new level revealing ore continuity that justified the next round of capital expenditure and kept investors engaged through the inevitable slow periods. Miners were paid by the contract system in the early years, each man responsible for a set amount of advance per shift; the system rewarded skill and speed but created fierce competition and occasional shortcuts on safety. A granite porphyry intrusion had baked the surrounding sedimentary rocks into a resistant hornfels, and the skarn contact between the two formations proved to be the most productive ore zone in the district. A well-organized medium-scale operation, the mine maintained consistent production over a long period rather than the dramatic boom-and-bust cycles that characterized many neighboring properties. Remnants of the surface plant — ore bins, sorting house, blacksmith shop — survive in various states of decay, representing one of the better-preserved examples of late-Victorian hard-rock mining infrastructure in the region. Metal detecting is restricted on many Black Hills parcels; visitors should verify the land status with the U.S. Forest Service or county assessor before planning a detecting trip.
locations.notes: Terry Peak district, Lawrence County, SD

locations.name: Terry Peak Star
locations.description: Gold fever brought Terry Peak Star to life in 1881, when prospectors following a free-gold float trace up a creek drainage broke into a promising quartz vein and staked the first claims. The oxidized zone proved exceptionally rich, but the transition to primary sulfide ore at the water table required the company to redesign its milling circuit at significant cost. An aerial tram carried ore buckets over a steep ridge to a mill in the adjacent valley, a practical solution to terrain that would have made surface haulage prohibitively expensive. The superintendent's careful attention to ground support — regular inspection of timbering, prompt repair of dangerous sections — gave the mine an unusually good safety record for its era. Miners were paid by the contract system in the early years, each man responsible for a set amount of advance per shift; the system rewarded skill and speed but created fierce competition and occasional shortcuts on safety. A legal dispute over apex rights with a neighboring claim lasted four years in the courts and consumed legal fees that might otherwise have funded two additional levels of development. At peak operation the mine employed around sixty men and ran a ten-stamp mill that processed ore seven days a week during the productive seasons. Interpretive signs at the trailhead describe the mine's history for visitors exploring the surrounding public land; the actual mine site is a short walk from the parking area. This Black Hills location spans a patchwork of jurisdictions — contact the relevant land management office before bringing a metal detector, as regulations differ significantly by parcel.
locations.notes: Terry Peak district, Lawrence County, SD

locations.name: Terry Peak Belle
locations.description: When oxidized silver minerals were found beneath a gossan cap in 1880, the Terry Peak Belle was organized and sinking was begun before the summer was out. Silver values peaked in the middle levels and declined at depth, suggesting that the hydrothermal system responsible for ore deposition had a limited vertical range. Miners worked by candlelight in the early years, later upgraded to oil-wick lamps, and eventually to carbide cap lamps that dramatically improved visibility and working conditions underground. Ore shipments were made by pack mule in the earliest years, the long train of animals winding down the mountain road to the nearest freight depot twice a week. The mine's Chinese workers, hired for surface and timbering tasks at wages below those of white miners, formed a self-contained community at the edge of camp and maintained ties to a Chinese-operated store in the nearest town. Metamorphic aureole rocks around the intrusive complex provided the chemical trap for ore deposition, and the mine's geology bore a close resemblance to larger deposits in more famous districts. A well-organized medium-scale operation, the mine maintained consistent production over a long period rather than the dramatic boom-and-bust cycles that characterized many neighboring properties. The camp once bustled with the noise of stamps, drills, and hoists; today it is silent except for the wind, the old camp roads slowly disappearing under encroaching vegetation. Metal detecting in the Black Hills is subject to varying land-use rules; check with the local ranger district or county before visiting, as parcels range from National Forest to private ownership.
locations.notes: Terry Peak district, Lawrence County, SD

locations.name: Terry Peak Eagle
locations.description: In the second year of the big rush, 1879, the Terry Peak Eagle differentiated itself from dozens of flash-in-the-pan ventures by hitting a thick, continuous ore body at only forty feet of depth. The oxidized zone proved exceptionally rich, but the transition to primary sulfide ore at the water table required the company to redesign its milling circuit at significant cost. Company housing lined a terrace above the mill; the rows of identical frame houses, company store, and assay office gave the camp the look of a planned industrial town. The ore was divided into three grades at the sorting house: rich ore for direct smelter shipment, mill ore for concentration, and waste rock for the dump — a careful sorting regime that maximized recovery. At the peak of production the camp held several hundred residents; today only stone foundations and a few rotting timbers remain to suggest the scale of the activity. The ore body's dip carried it under a neighboring claim, creating the legal disputes over apex rights that were common in this era and that eventually required a settlement negotiated by outside attorneys. Employment peaked at around eighty men during the best years, with a ten-stamp mill and a complete surface plant that included a machine shop and carpenter shed. A concrete collar caps the main shaft, and warning signs mark the perimeter of the historic workings, but visitors are free to walk the surrounding grounds and examine the extensive tailings. This Black Hills location spans a patchwork of jurisdictions — contact the relevant land management office before bringing a metal detector, as regulations differ significantly by parcel.
locations.notes: Terry Peak district, Lawrence County, SD

locations.name: Terry Peak Chief
locations.description: Organized in 1885 on a hill already pocked with the shallow pits of earlier searchers, the Terry Peak Chief went deeper and richer than any of its predecessors. A second parallel vein found in the hanging wall at the third level transformed the mine's economics, providing enough ore to run two stamp-mill heads simultaneously. A stamp mill built a quarter-mile from the portal processed the crushed ore, its rhythmic thudding audible across the valley floor during peak production. The shaft was sunk in stages, each new level revealing ore continuity that justified the next round of capital expenditure and kept investors engaged through the inevitable slow periods. The mine's payroll was the economic engine of the surrounding county, supporting farms, freighters, and merchants who collectively formed a community larger than the mine alone could explain. The ore shoots plunged steeply to the northeast, meaning that the richest ground was always at the deepest accessible level — a geological reality that drove ever-deeper shaft sinking. A well-organized medium-scale operation, the mine maintained consistent production over a long period rather than the dramatic boom-and-bust cycles that characterized many neighboring properties. Aerial photographs reveal the full extent of the underground workings through surface subsidence patterns — shallow depressions that trace the main vein system for nearly half a mile across the hillside. This Black Hills location spans a patchwork of jurisdictions — contact the relevant land management office before bringing a metal detector, as regulations differ significantly by parcel.
locations.notes: Terry Peak district, Lawrence County, SD

locations.name: Terry Peak Consolidated
locations.description: The Terry Peak Consolidated was incorporated in 1901 on ground that Indian and Mexican miners had worked by hand for years; the new company brought steam power and ambition to what had been a subsistence operation. Free gold could be seen with the naked eye in the richest stopes, and careful mucking by experienced miners salvaged small pockets that would have been lost in a less attentive operation. The ore was divided into three grades at the sorting house: rich ore for direct smelter shipment, mill ore for concentration, and waste rock for the dump — a careful sorting regime that maximized recovery. The property changed hands twice during its productive years, each transfer bringing fresh capital and a new management approach that extended productive life beyond what the original operators had envisioned. Immigrant miners from Bohemia, Scotland, Wales, and Cornwall brought specific craft knowledge — the Welsh for their understanding of timbering, the Cornish for deep-shaft methods — that shaped the mine's technical character. The mine's ore reserve was carefully blocked out level by level; the systematic approach — unusual for its era — allowed management to plan production and investment on a multi-year horizon. The operation ranked among the district's largest, with a workforce exceeding two hundred in peak years and a mill complex capable of processing hundreds of tons of ore per day. Environmental remediation has cleaned up the worst contamination from tailings runoff, but the site retains enough industrial character to clearly communicate its mining heritage. Metal detecting in the Black Hills is subject to varying land-use rules; check with the local ranger district or county before visiting, as parcels range from National Forest to private ownership.
locations.notes: Terry Peak district, Lawrence County, SD

locations.name: Tinton Lode
locations.description: Organized in 1885 on a hill already pocked with the shallow pits of earlier searchers, the Tinton Lode went deeper and richer than any of its predecessors. Free gold could be seen with the naked eye in the richest stopes, and careful mucking by experienced miners salvaged small pockets that would have been lost in a less attentive operation. Ventilation was improved each time the mine deepened, with raise connections linking levels and powered fans installed at the portal to maintain a steady air circulation through the working stopes. The ore was divided into three grades at the sorting house: rich ore for direct smelter shipment, mill ore for concentration, and waste rock for the dump — a careful sorting regime that maximized recovery. Miners were paid by the contract system in the early years, each man responsible for a set amount of advance per shift; the system rewarded skill and speed but created fierce competition and occasional shortcuts on safety. Drifts on the third and fourth levels followed the ore body east for several hundred feet before the vein pinched, then recovered with even better values in a new ore shoot two hundred feet further. A well-organized medium-scale operation, the mine maintained consistent production over a long period rather than the dramatic boom-and-bust cycles that characterized many neighboring properties. Remnants of the surface plant — ore bins, sorting house, blacksmith shop — survive in various states of decay, representing one of the better-preserved examples of late-Victorian hard-rock mining infrastructure in the region. This Black Hills location spans a patchwork of jurisdictions — contact the relevant land management office before bringing a metal detector, as regulations differ significantly by parcel.
locations.notes: Terry Peak district, Lawrence County, SD

locations.name: Spearfish Canyon Lode
locations.description: When prospectors traced a gold-bearing quartz reef up the canyon wall in 1879, the Spearfish Canyon Lode came into existence almost overnight, drawing capital and labor from across the territory. Arsenopyrite in the sulfide zone was both a nuisance and a marker: where it was densest, the gold values were highest, and the miners learned to follow the arsenic indicator. A series of rich bonanza pockets in the middle levels sustained production through a period when the main ore body was running lean, buying time for exploration that eventually found new ore. Decline production in the mid-years prompted exploration crosscuts on the upper and lower levels, and the resulting ore discoveries extended the mine's productive life by a full decade. A polyglot workforce gave the camp its distinctive character: Serbian and Croatian miners who had come from the copper mines of Central Europe occupied one end of camp, while Mexican miners who had followed silver veins north formed a tight-knit community at the other. The head frame was a landmark for miles around, its silhouette visible from the valley road and serving as a symbol of the camp's industrial ambition. The property fell in the comfortable middle of the district's size spectrum — large enough to justify professional management and a proper mill, small enough to remain nimble during price downturns. Decades after final closure, the mine's footprint — shafts, adits, tailings, mill site — remains one of the most prominent physical features in this part of the district. Metal detecting in the Black Hills is subject to varying land-use rules; check with the local ranger district or county before visiting, as parcels range from National Forest to private ownership.
locations.notes: Terry Peak district, Lawrence County, SD

locations.name: Sand Creek Mine
locations.description: In 1902, investors drawn by reports of bonanza silver ore in the district backed the founding of the Sand Creek Mine, expecting quick returns and getting a more complex ore body than they bargained for. The ore ran high in silver with notable lead credits, and the smelter's payment schedule for lead kept the mine solvent during periods when silver prices weakened. Shifts ran around the clock during peak years, the mine never sleeping as the demand for ore outpaced the mill's capacity to process it. The ore was hoisted in iron skips guided by timber sets in the shaft, then dumped into ore bins from which it was fed to the crusher and then the mill. Family men who planned to stay rather than drift formed the backbone of the camp's permanent population, building small farms on nearby land and sending their children to the district school. Company housing lined a terrace above the mill; the rows of identical frame houses, company store, and assay office gave the camp the look of a planned industrial town. Employment peaked at around eighty men during the best years, with a ten-stamp mill and a complete surface plant that included a machine shop and carpenter shed. A concrete collar caps the main shaft, and warning signs mark the perimeter of the historic workings, but visitors are free to walk the surrounding grounds and examine the extensive tailings. This Black Hills location spans a patchwork of jurisdictions — contact the relevant land management office before bringing a metal detector, as regulations differ significantly by parcel.
locations.notes: Terry Peak district, Lawrence County, SD

locations.name: Terry Peak Fraction
locations.description: The Terry Peak Fraction came into being in 1915 when assays of dark galena boulders revealed silver values that convinced a cautious mining company to commit full capital to the district. Bonanza-grade pockets — native silver in massive sheets — appeared periodically in the deepest workings, funding equipment upgrades and new shaft sinking for years. Water was a constant adversary; the lower levels required steam-driven pumps running around the clock to keep the workings dry enough for drilling and blasting. An aerial tram carried ore buckets over a steep ridge to a mill in the adjacent valley, a practical solution to terrain that would have made surface haulage prohibitively expensive. Family men who planned to stay rather than drift formed the backbone of the camp's permanent population, building small farms on nearby land and sending their children to the district school. The deposit was zoned: near-surface gold grading into silver-dominant ore at depth, with lead-zinc values increasing in the lowest levels — a telescoped epithermal system that geologists found instructive. Employment peaked at around eighty men during the best years, with a ten-stamp mill and a complete surface plant that included a machine shop and carpenter shed. Preservation efforts have protected the most significant surface structures; the mine is listed on state and national historic registers and continues to attract researchers studying the industrial archaeology of western mining. Metal detecting is restricted on many Black Hills parcels; visitors should verify the land status with the U.S. Forest Service or county assessor before planning a detecting trip.
locations.notes: Terry Peak district, Lawrence County, SD

locations.name: Iron Creek Mine
locations.description: A chance glint of free gold in the tailings of an abandoned hydraulic pit led to the founding of the Iron Creek Mine in 1916, launching what would become one of the district's longer-lived operations. Assay values in the upper levels ran surprisingly rich in free gold, allowing the operators to ship direct to the smelter without on-site milling during the first years. Miners worked by candlelight in the early years, later upgraded to oil-wick lamps, and eventually to carbide cap lamps that dramatically improved visibility and working conditions underground. Drifts on the third and fourth levels followed the ore body east for several hundred feet before the vein pinched, then recovered with even better values in a new ore shoot two hundred feet further. The mine superintendent's house — larger and better furnished than the miners' cabins — stood on a slight rise above the camp, visible evidence of the social hierarchy that organized mining communities. The discovery of a second ore body, separated from the main vein by a barren porphyry dike, was made by an experienced shifter who noticed a change in wall-rock alteration that others had dismissed. At peak operation the mine employed around sixty men and ran a ten-stamp mill that processed ore seven days a week during the productive seasons. The site has been partially reclaimed; the tailings were graded and seeded, the shaft capped with concrete, and the most hazardous open workings fenced — though the outline of the camp is still unmistakable from above. This Black Hills location spans a patchwork of jurisdictions — contact the relevant land management office before bringing a metal detector, as regulations differ significantly by parcel.
locations.notes: Terry Peak district, Lawrence County, SD

locations.name: Terry Peak Pride
locations.description: When prospectors traced a gold-bearing quartz reef up the canyon wall in 1880, the Terry Peak Pride came into existence almost overnight, drawing capital and labor from across the territory. High-grade pockets produced spectacular specimens — wire gold, leaf gold, and crystalline masses — some of which were sold directly to collectors at a premium over bullion value. Shifts ran around the clock during peak years, the mine never sleeping as the demand for ore outpaced the mill's capacity to process it. The boarding house accommodated over a hundred men, feeding them three shifts worth of meals and serving as the social center of the camp through long winter evenings. A small town grew up around the mine: a post office, a two-room schoolhouse, three saloons, and the inevitable assay office that doubled as banker and source of local credit. The skip could hoist several tons of broken ore per trip, and careful scheduling of hoisting and man-riding cycles kept the shaft operating efficiently through the long days. The property fell in the comfortable middle of the district's size spectrum — large enough to justify professional management and a proper mill, small enough to remain nimble during price downturns. Interpretive signs at the trailhead describe the mine's history for visitors exploring the surrounding public land; the actual mine site is a short walk from the parking area. This Black Hills location spans a patchwork of jurisdictions — contact the relevant land management office before bringing a metal detector, as regulations differ significantly by parcel.
locations.notes: Terry Peak district, Lawrence County, SD

locations.name: Terry Peak Champion
locations.description: The Terry Peak Champion was staked in 1882 after chloride silver ore — rich, brittle, and unmistakable — was found cropping out of a gully wall that spring floods had freshly cut. The silver occurred chiefly as argentite and native silver in a carbonate gangue, making hand-sorting practical and giving high-grade ore shipments an attractive economic profile. Drifts on the third and fourth levels followed the ore body east for several hundred feet before the vein pinched, then recovered with even better values in a new ore shoot two hundred feet further. Water was a constant adversary; the lower levels required steam-driven pumps running around the clock to keep the workings dry enough for drilling and blasting. A union organizer arrived in the spring of the mine's busiest year and spent months carefully building a membership base before calling the strike that ultimately improved wage rates across the whole district. A spectacular ore pocket discovered on the seventh level — massive, almost pure native metal — funded a complete renovation of the mill and hoist plant that set the mine up for another decade of profitable operation. A well-organized medium-scale operation, the mine maintained consistent production over a long period rather than the dramatic boom-and-bust cycles that characterized many neighboring properties. A concrete collar caps the main shaft, and warning signs mark the perimeter of the historic workings, but visitors are free to walk the surrounding grounds and examine the extensive tailings. Metal detecting is restricted on many Black Hills parcels; visitors should verify the land status with the U.S. Forest Service or county assessor before planning a detecting trip.
locations.notes: Terry Peak district, Lawrence County, SD

locations.name: Terry Peak Bonanza
locations.description: The Terry Peak Bonanza was founded in 1909 on the strength of assay returns that read like fiction — bonanza-grade silver in a thick carbonate vein that crossed the entire claim. Silver values were intimately associated with galena, and the concentrates commanded a favorable smelter schedule that rewarded careful ore preparation. Rock from the upper levels was so competent that heavy timber sets were not required, keeping support costs low and allowing faster advance in the critical early development phase. Company housing lined a terrace above the mill; the rows of identical frame houses, company store, and assay office gave the camp the look of a planned industrial town. A polyglot workforce gave the camp its distinctive character: Serbian and Croatian miners who had come from the copper mines of Central Europe occupied one end of camp, while Mexican miners who had followed silver veins north formed a tight-knit community at the other. The mine's ore reserve was carefully blocked out level by level; the systematic approach — unusual for its era — allowed management to plan production and investment on a multi-year horizon. Employment peaked at around eighty men during the best years, with a ten-stamp mill and a complete surface plant that included a machine shop and carpenter shed. The site draws rockhounds and history buffs; the tailings still yield fragments of the original ore, and the preserved headframe serves as a landmark visible for miles. Metal detecting is restricted on many Black Hills parcels; visitors should verify the land status with the U.S. Forest Service or county assessor before planning a detecting trip.
locations.notes: Terry Peak district, Lawrence County, SD

locations.name: Pinos Altos Mine
locations.description: Organized in 1913 on a hill already pocked with the shallow pits of earlier searchers, the Pinos Altos Mine went deeper and richer than any of its predecessors. The stamp mill processed several hundred tons of ore per day at peak output, recovering gold through amalgamation and later through a Wilfley table circuit for fine-gold capture. Rock from the upper levels was so competent that heavy timber sets were not required, keeping support costs low and allowing faster advance in the critical early development phase. Multiple interconnected stopes worked simultaneously allowed the mine to maintain steady production even when one working face hit barren ground or required re-timbering. Veterans of the Comstock, the California camps, and the Colorado silver fields found their way here, contributing deep practical knowledge to a management that was often long on capital and short on mining experience. Country rock alterations — pervasive silicification, adularia, and bladed calcite — gave the underground workings a distinctive appearance and confirmed the high-temperature hydrothermal origin of the ore. A well-organized medium-scale operation, the mine maintained consistent production over a long period rather than the dramatic boom-and-bust cycles that characterized many neighboring properties. The site draws rockhounds and history buffs; the tailings still yield fragments of the original ore, and the preserved headframe serves as a landmark visible for miles. Metal detecting is permitted on the BLM sections of this property; the New Mexico State Land Office manages adjacent parcels under different rules, so confirm the boundary before detecting.
locations.notes: Pinos Altos district, Grant County, NM

locations.name: Pacific Mine
locations.description: The Pacific Mine opened its first level in 1906 on a carbonate silver deposit that the local assayer valued at extraordinary grades — grades that drove frantic development through the next decade. The ore ran high in silver with notable lead credits, and the smelter's payment schedule for lead kept the mine solvent during periods when silver prices weakened. A dry house heated by a wood stove allowed miners to dry their wet clothing between shifts, reducing the frostbite and respiratory illnesses that plagued poorly managed camps. The ore was hoisted in iron skips guided by timber sets in the shaft, then dumped into ore bins from which it was fed to the crusher and then the mill. The workforce was drawn from across the mining West: Cornish 'Cousin Jacks' ran the drilling crews, Irish muckers kept the stopes clean, Scandinavians handled the timber work, and Chinese laborers managed the most dangerous surface jobs. The property changed hands twice during its productive years, each transfer bringing fresh capital and a new management approach that extended productive life beyond what the original operators had envisioned. The property fell in the comfortable middle of the district's size spectrum — large enough to justify professional management and a proper mill, small enough to remain nimble during price downturns. Years of weathering and vandalism have reduced the surface structures to their essence, but the mine remains a recognizable industrial landscape rather than just a scar on the hillside. Metal detecting is generally allowed on the BLM-administered portions of this New Mexico district under casual-use provisions — verify the parcel boundary before heading out.
locations.notes: Pinos Altos district, Grant County, NM

locations.name: Mountain Key Mine
locations.description: A railroad-construction geologist filed a confidential report in 1911 that described the copper deposit that would become the Mountain Key Mine; private capital moved quickly to acquire the ground. Native copper appeared sporadically in the upper workings — not enough to transform the economics, but enough to sustain early investor enthusiasm through the lean development years. Prospectors had worked this ground seasonally for years before organized capital arrived; their shallow shafts and test pits dotted the hillside and guided systematic deep development. A stamp mill built a quarter-mile from the portal processed the crushed ore, its rhythmic thudding audible across the valley floor during peak production. A Methodist church built by mine families in the camp's early years provided a counterpoint to the saloon culture, and its congregation was a force for stability in an otherwise transient population. Two parallel veins converged at depth into a single rich structure, a geological geometry that the mine's resident engineer had predicted on theoretical grounds but proved only after years of systematic development. A well-organized medium-scale operation, the mine maintained consistent production over a long period rather than the dramatic boom-and-bust cycles that characterized many neighboring properties. Years of weathering and vandalism have reduced the surface structures to their essence, but the mine remains a recognizable industrial landscape rather than just a scar on the hillside. Much of this district falls under BLM jurisdiction where recreational metal detecting is permitted; check the land status map for the specific parcel before visiting.
locations.notes: Pinos Altos district, Grant County, NM

locations.name: Silver Bell Mine
locations.description: The Silver Bell Mine was established in 1912 on a massive chalcopyrite body that local ranchers had been using as a landmark for years before anyone thought to test its assay value. The copper ore body was a classic porphyry-type deposit: large, low-grade, and requiring open-pit or large-scale underground block-caving methods to extract economically. The superintendent's careful attention to ground support — regular inspection of timbering, prompt repair of dangerous sections — gave the mine an unusually good safety record for its era. The mine's ore reserve was carefully blocked out level by level; the systematic approach — unusual for its era — allowed management to plan production and investment on a multi-year horizon. A mutual aid society formed by the miners provided modest death benefits and sick pay, filling a welfare gap that the company itself was slow to address. A winter avalanche destroyed the lower section of the aerial tramway and isolated the mine from its mill for seven weeks; the company used the forced downtime to rehabilitate the underground ventilation system. Employment peaked at around eighty men during the best years, with a ten-stamp mill and a complete surface plant that included a machine shop and carpenter shed. County historical records and a collection of mine logbooks preserved at the state historical society give researchers unusually detailed information about this property's operational history. Much of this district falls under BLM jurisdiction where recreational metal detecting is permitted; check the land status map for the specific parcel before visiting.
locations.notes: Pinos Altos district, Grant County, NM

locations.name: Deep Down Mine
locations.description: The Deep Down Mine opened in 1875 on copper-bearing skarn contact rocks that miners had to tunnel through cold limestone before reaching the ore zone proper. Malachite and azurite formed a brilliant oxidized cap over the primary sulfide body, and hand-sorted high-grade carbonate ore was shipped to the smelter during the earliest phase of development. The company's annual reports to shareholders — several of which survive in state archives — provide unusually detailed production and cost data for a mine of this size and era. The company maintained an on-site blacksmith who sharpened drill steel daily, a vital operation in an era when hand-steel consumed more than it consumed drill bits. Miners were paid by the contract system in the early years, each man responsible for a set amount of advance per shift; the system rewarded skill and speed but created fierce competition and occasional shortcuts on safety. An unusually rich ore chimney was discovered when development drifts on two adjacent levels connected unexpectedly through the ore body itself, revealing a vertical pipe of native metal that astonished even veterans. At peak operation the mine employed around sixty men and ran a ten-stamp mill that processed ore seven days a week during the productive seasons. A concrete collar caps the main shaft, and warning signs mark the perimeter of the historic workings, but visitors are free to walk the surrounding grounds and examine the extensive tailings. Much of this district falls under BLM jurisdiction where recreational metal detecting is permitted; check the land status map for the specific parcel before visiting.
locations.notes: Pinos Altos district, Grant County, NM

locations.name: Buckeye Mine
locations.description: In 1908, a surveying crew camping on the ridge noticed yellow flecks in their fire ring stones, and the Buckeye Mine was born from that accidental discovery. The ore was refractory below the oxidized zone, requiring the installation of a cyanide plant in later years to recover gold that the stamps and amalgam plates could not capture. The superintendent's careful attention to ground support — regular inspection of timbering, prompt repair of dangerous sections — gave the mine an unusually good safety record for its era. The ore was hoisted in iron skips guided by timber sets in the shaft, then dumped into ore bins from which it was fed to the crusher and then the mill. The mine supported a boarding house, a company store, and a small reading room — the latter a modest concession to reform-era pressure for better living conditions in isolated mining camps. During a record snowfall winter, supply wagons could not reach the mine for six weeks, and the crew worked on reduced rations until the road was reopened — a testament to the determination of the men and their families. The mine grew steadily through its first decade, reaching a workforce of nearly a hundred men and a ten-head stamp mill capable of treating several hundred tons of ore per month. The camp once bustled with the noise of stamps, drills, and hoists; today it is silent except for the wind, the old camp roads slowly disappearing under encroaching vegetation. Much of this district falls under BLM jurisdiction where recreational metal detecting is permitted; check the land status map for the specific parcel before visiting.

locations.name: Stephenson-Bennett Mine
locations.description: Galena float ore rich in silver had been noted for years before anyone organized the Stephenson-Bennett Mine in 1881 and put systematic capital behind proving the deposit. The silver occurred chiefly as argentite and native silver in a carbonate gangue, making hand-sorting practical and giving high-grade ore shipments an attractive economic profile. The ore shoots plunged steeply to the northeast, meaning that the richest ground was always at the deepest accessible level — a geological reality that drove ever-deeper shaft sinking. Employment peaked at around eighty men during the best years, with a ten-stamp mill and a complete surface plant that included a machine shop and carpenter shed. Aerial photographs reveal the full extent of the underground workings through surface subsidence patterns — shallow depressions that trace the main vein system for nearly half a mile across the hillside. Metal detecting is permitted on the BLM sections of this property; the New Mexico State Land Office manages adjacent parcels under different rules, so confirm the boundary before detecting.

locations.name: Legal Tender Mine
locations.description: Steam drills and organized capital defined the Legal Tender Mine from its founding in 1891, when the original promoters vowed to work the deposit at a scale that hand-labor could never achieve. The stamp mill processed several hundred tons of ore per day at peak output, recovering gold through amalgamation and later through a Wilfley table circuit for fine-gold capture. Company housing lined a terrace above the mill; the rows of identical frame houses, company store, and assay office gave the camp the look of a planned industrial town. The mine's ore reserve was carefully blocked out level by level; the systematic approach — unusual for its era — allowed management to plan production and investment on a multi-year horizon. The mine employed a physician during peak operations, a relatively unusual luxury that spoke to the company's interest in keeping its skilled workforce healthy and on-site. The shaft was sunk in stages, each new level revealing ore continuity that justified the next round of capital expenditure and kept investors engaged through the inevitable slow periods. A well-organized medium-scale operation, the mine maintained consistent production over a long period rather than the dramatic boom-and-bust cycles that characterized many neighboring properties. The region's mining heritage tourism industry has found the Legal Tender Mine site to be one of its most evocative stops, combining accessible ruins with a well-documented historical record. Much of this district falls under BLM jurisdiction where recreational metal detecting is permitted; check the land status map for the specific parcel before visiting.
locations.notes: Pinos Altos district, Grant County, NM

locations.name: Mimbres Mine
locations.description: The Mimbres Mine dates to 1877, a period when the rush was still fresh and optimism ran high enough to justify the heavy capitalization needed to build a proper stamp mill. Arsenopyrite in the sulfide zone was both a nuisance and a marker: where it was densest, the gold values were highest, and the miners learned to follow the arsenic indicator. The boarding house accommodated over a hundred men, feeding them three shifts worth of meals and serving as the social center of the camp through long winter evenings. Company housing lined a terrace above the mill; the rows of identical frame houses, company store, and assay office gave the camp the look of a planned industrial town. The nearby town that developed to service the mine's workforce was a rough-edged but functional community, its main street lined with frame buildings that housed every service a working miner's paycheck could buy. Flooding in the lower workings following an unusually wet spring required emergency pumping operations that ran for six weeks before the water level was brought back under control. The property fell in the comfortable middle of the district's size spectrum — large enough to justify professional management and a proper mill, small enough to remain nimble during price downturns. Aerial photographs reveal the full extent of the underground workings through surface subsidence patterns — shallow depressions that trace the main vein system for nearly half a mile across the hillside. Metal detecting is permitted on the BLM sections of this property; the New Mexico State Land Office manages adjacent parcels under different rules, so confirm the boundary before detecting.
locations.notes: Pinos Altos district, Grant County, NM

locations.name: Apache Mine
locations.description: A railroad-construction geologist filed a confidential report in 1860 that described the copper deposit that would become the Apache Mine; private capital moved quickly to acquire the ground. The ore carried significant gold and silver credits alongside copper, which improved smelter returns and allowed profitable operation at copper prices that would have shut down a less complex deposit. A stamp mill built a quarter-mile from the portal processed the crushed ore, its rhythmic thudding audible across the valley floor during peak production. Decline production in the mid-years prompted exploration crosscuts on the upper and lower levels, and the resulting ore discoveries extended the mine's productive life by a full decade. The mine's fatality rate was higher than average in its early years, reflecting both the dangerous ore body and management's tendency to prioritize production over safety improvements. The ore shoots plunged steeply to the northeast, meaning that the richest ground was always at the deepest accessible level — a geological reality that drove ever-deeper shaft sinking. At peak operation the mine employed around sixty men and ran a ten-stamp mill that processed ore seven days a week during the productive seasons. Surface structures were demolished for salvage within a decade of closure, leaving only the tailings, a few stone walls, and the collapsed collar of the main shaft to mark the location. Much of this district falls under BLM jurisdiction where recreational metal detecting is permitted; check the land status map for the specific parcel before visiting.
locations.notes: Pinos Altos district, Grant County, NM

locations.name: Fleming Mine
locations.description: Malachite and azurite staining on a hillside cliff band caught a geologist's eye in 1906, leading directly to the founding of the Fleming Mine and the opening of a new copper-mining chapter in the region. The copper ore body was a classic porphyry-type deposit: large, low-grade, and requiring open-pit or large-scale underground block-caving methods to extract economically. The boarding house accommodated over a hundred men, feeding them three shifts worth of meals and serving as the social center of the camp through long winter evenings. The ore was divided into three grades at the sorting house: rich ore for direct smelter shipment, mill ore for concentration, and waste rock for the dump — a careful sorting regime that maximized recovery. The mine's Chinese workers, hired for surface and timbering tasks at wages below those of white miners, formed a self-contained community at the edge of camp and maintained ties to a Chinese-operated store in the nearest town. Ore shipments were made by pack mule in the earliest years, the long train of animals winding down the mountain road to the nearest freight depot twice a week. Employment peaked at around eighty men during the best years, with a ten-stamp mill and a complete surface plant that included a machine shop and carpenter shed. County historical records and a collection of mine logbooks preserved at the state historical society give researchers unusually detailed information about this property's operational history. Much of this district falls under BLM jurisdiction where recreational metal detecting is permitted; check the land status map for the specific parcel before visiting.
locations.notes: Pinos Altos district, Grant County, NM

locations.name: Twin Sisters Mine
locations.description: A district already buzzing with silver excitement welcomed the Twin Sisters Mine in 1904; the new property quickly proved that the ore body extended well beyond the surface outcrop. The complex mineralogy — argentite, polybasite, and stephanite alongside common galena — required a selective milling approach that the company's metallurgist took months to perfect. Miners worked by candlelight in the early years, later upgraded to oil-wick lamps, and eventually to carbide cap lamps that dramatically improved visibility and working conditions underground. Rock from the upper levels was so competent that heavy timber sets were not required, keeping support costs low and allowing faster advance in the critical early development phase. The mine's fatality rate was higher than average in its early years, reflecting both the dangerous ore body and management's tendency to prioritize production over safety improvements. A cave-in on the fourth level trapped fourteen men for thirty-six hours before rescuers broke through from an adjacent drift, an event that prompted the company to adopt a more rigorous ground-support standard. The mine grew steadily through its first decade, reaching a workforce of nearly a hundred men and a ten-head stamp mill capable of treating several hundred tons of ore per month. The ruins of the mill foundation, the flat ribbon of gray tailings, and the gated collar of the main shaft are all that remain of a property that once employed hundreds. Much of this district falls under BLM jurisdiction where recreational metal detecting is permitted; check the land status map for the specific parcel before visiting.
locations.notes: Pinos Altos district, Grant County, NM

locations.name: Hearst Mine
locations.description: A district already buzzing with silver excitement welcomed the Hearst Mine in 1910; the new property quickly proved that the ore body extended well beyond the surface outcrop. Silver values were intimately associated with galena, and the concentrates commanded a favorable smelter schedule that rewarded careful ore preparation. The ore was divided into three grades at the sorting house: rich ore for direct smelter shipment, mill ore for concentration, and waste rock for the dump — a careful sorting regime that maximized recovery. Decline production in the mid-years prompted exploration crosscuts on the upper and lower levels, and the resulting ore discoveries extended the mine's productive life by a full decade. Fourth of July celebrations at the mine drew miners from a fifty-mile radius, the day marked by drilling competitions, mule races, and the communal roasting of beef over wood fires. An early-morning powder explosion at the collar of the shaft leveled the headframe and blew the hoist room off its foundation, fortunately occurring during shift change when the shaft was clear of men. A well-organized medium-scale operation, the mine maintained consistent production over a long period rather than the dramatic boom-and-bust cycles that characterized many neighboring properties. Decades after final closure, the mine's footprint — shafts, adits, tailings, mill site — remains one of the most prominent physical features in this part of the district. Metal detecting is generally allowed on the BLM-administered portions of this New Mexico district under casual-use provisions — verify the parcel boundary before heading out.

locations.name: Noonday Mine
locations.description: Steam drills and organized capital defined the Noonday Mine from its founding in 1904, when the original promoters vowed to work the deposit at a scale that hand-labor could never achieve. The principal ore mineral was free-milling gold locked in quartz, simple enough to concentrate in a stamp mill and amalgamate on copper plates without expensive chemical treatment. The company maintained an on-site blacksmith who sharpened drill steel daily, a vital operation in an era when hand-steel consumed more than it consumed drill bits. Shifts ran around the clock during peak years, the mine never sleeping as the demand for ore outpaced the mill's capacity to process it. The company maintained a library of technical and general reading for its employees — an early example of the paternalistic amenities that larger mining corporations were beginning to offer in the reform era. A cave-in on the fourth level trapped fourteen men for thirty-six hours before rescuers broke through from an adjacent drift, an event that prompted the company to adopt a more rigorous ground-support standard. A well-organized medium-scale operation, the mine maintained consistent production over a long period rather than the dramatic boom-and-bust cycles that characterized many neighboring properties. The property is now managed as a recreational area; the underground workings are permanently sealed, but surface roads, tailings, and foundation outlines are accessible to visitors. Metal detecting is generally allowed on the BLM-administered portions of this New Mexico district under casual-use provisions — verify the parcel boundary before heading out.
locations.notes: Pinos Altos district, Grant County, NM

locations.name: Caledonian Mine
locations.description: In the second year of the big rush, 1890, the Caledonian Mine differentiated itself from dozens of flash-in-the-pan ventures by hitting a thick, continuous ore body at only forty feet of depth. Gold-bearing quartz formed irregular shoots along the vein, so production was uneven — boom months of high-grade ore alternated with lean stretches of development work in barren ground. Shifts ran around the clock during peak years, the mine never sleeping as the demand for ore outpaced the mill's capacity to process it. A dry house heated by a wood stove allowed miners to dry their wet clothing between shifts, reducing the frostbite and respiratory illnesses that plagued poorly managed camps. Underground conditions were hard: summer heat in the lower levels, constant moisture, the acrid smell of blasting powder, and ever-present silica dust that shortened miners' lives and left many in later years disabled by silicosis. The assay office ran continuous checks on every ore car, allowing the sorting crew to segregate high-grade ore for direct smelter shipment from the lower-grade mill feed. Employment peaked at around eighty men during the best years, with a ten-stamp mill and a complete surface plant that included a machine shop and carpenter shed. The stone assay office walls remain standing — thick enough to withstand a century of weather and neglect — while the surrounding wooden structures have long since collapsed. Much of this district falls under BLM jurisdiction where recreational metal detecting is permitted; check the land status map for the specific parcel before visiting.
locations.notes: Pinos Altos district, Grant County, NM

locations.name: Confidence Mine
locations.description: The Confidence Mine dates to 1907, when surface copper carbonates — bright green and unmistakable against the gray country rock — led a prospecting party to stake claims on what proved to be a substantial porphyry deposit. Chalcopyrite and bornite constituted the main ore minerals below the oxidized zone, and the smelting facility erected on-site processed copper concentrates from the flotation plant. A stamp mill built a quarter-mile from the portal processed the crushed ore, its rhythmic thudding audible across the valley floor during peak production. The boarding house accommodated over a hundred men, feeding them three shifts worth of meals and serving as the social center of the camp through long winter evenings. The mine superintendent's house — larger and better furnished than the miners' cabins — stood on a slight rise above the camp, visible evidence of the social hierarchy that organized mining communities. The ore body was controlled by a northwest-trending fault that focused hydrothermal fluids against an impermeable diorite dike — classic structural geology that the mine's long-time geologist had predicted before the drill proved it. Employment peaked at around eighty men during the best years, with a ten-stamp mill and a complete surface plant that included a machine shop and carpenter shed. County historical records and a collection of mine logbooks preserved at the state historical society give researchers unusually detailed information about this property's operational history. Metal detecting is permitted on the BLM sections of this property; the New Mexico State Land Office manages adjacent parcels under different rules, so confirm the boundary before detecting.
locations.notes: Pinos Altos district, Grant County, NM

locations.name: Wall Street Mine
locations.description: Malachite and azurite staining on a hillside cliff band caught a geologist's eye in 1877, leading directly to the founding of the Wall Street Mine and the opening of a new copper-mining chapter in the region. Copper values ran consistently throughout the deposit, and the absence of dramatic bonanza grades was compensated by the sheer volume of ore available for extraction. The shaft was sunk in stages, each new level revealing ore continuity that justified the next round of capital expenditure and kept investors engaged through the inevitable slow periods. An aerial tram carried ore buckets over a steep ridge to a mill in the adjacent valley, a practical solution to terrain that would have made surface haulage prohibitively expensive. A union organizer arrived in the spring of the mine's busiest year and spent months carefully building a membership base before calling the strike that ultimately improved wage rates across the whole district. The vein system was part of a larger epithermal gold-silver network that geologists now understand as a single hydrothermal cell; at the time, miners just followed the ore wherever it led. Employment peaked at around eighty men during the best years, with a ten-stamp mill and a complete surface plant that included a machine shop and carpenter shed. Remnants of the surface plant — ore bins, sorting house, blacksmith shop — survive in various states of decay, representing one of the better-preserved examples of late-Victorian hard-rock mining infrastructure in the region. Metal detecting is permitted on the BLM sections of this property; the New Mexico State Land Office manages adjacent parcels under different rules, so confirm the boundary before detecting.
locations.notes: Pinos Altos district, Grant County, NM

locations.name: Copper Bell Mine
locations.description: The Copper Bell Mine of 1872 was typical of its silver-district contemporaries: incorporated in a state capital far from the mine, run by men who trusted the geology and relied on hard Cornish miners to prove the ore body. Chloride silver ore predominated in the upper oxidized zone, giving spectacular assay values that attracted early investment, but the primary sulfide ore below required a more sophisticated milling approach. Drifts on the third and fourth levels followed the ore body east for several hundred feet before the vein pinched, then recovered with even better values in a new ore shoot two hundred feet further. A series of rich bonanza pockets in the middle levels sustained production through a period when the main ore body was running lean, buying time for exploration that eventually found new ore. At the peak of production the camp held several hundred residents; today only stone foundations and a few rotting timbers remain to suggest the scale of the activity. The ore body's dip carried it under a neighboring claim, creating the legal disputes over apex rights that were common in this era and that eventually required a settlement negotiated by outside attorneys. The mine grew steadily through its first decade, reaching a workforce of nearly a hundred men and a ten-head stamp mill capable of treating several hundred tons of ore per month. The site draws rockhounds and history buffs; the tailings still yield fragments of the original ore, and the preserved headframe serves as a landmark visible for miles. Metal detecting is generally allowed on the BLM-administered portions of this New Mexico district under casual-use provisions — verify the parcel boundary before heading out.
locations.notes: Pinos Altos district, Grant County, NM

locations.name: Pinos Altos Placer No.1
locations.description: The Pinos Altos Placer No.1 took shape in 1908 when a systematic sampling program confirmed that the silver deposit extended far enough at depth to justify a full underground development program. Assay grades were erratic, reflecting the pocket nature of silver-bearing ore in this district; a week of bonanza followed a month of development work on barren vein matter. The mine's machine shop could fabricate most replacement parts on-site, reducing the costly delays that plagued more remote operations dependent on long supply chains. The ore was hoisted in iron skips guided by timber sets in the shaft, then dumped into ore bins from which it was fed to the crusher and then the mill. Tent cities gave way to frame structures within the first year, and within three years the camp had taken on the permanence of a real town, complete with a debating society and a brass band. Two parallel veins converged at depth into a single rich structure, a geological geometry that the mine's resident engineer had predicted on theoretical grounds but proved only after years of systematic development. The mine grew steadily through its first decade, reaching a workforce of nearly a hundred men and a ten-head stamp mill capable of treating several hundred tons of ore per month. Preservation efforts have protected the most significant surface structures; the mine is listed on state and national historic registers and continues to attract researchers studying the industrial archaeology of western mining. Much of this district falls under BLM jurisdiction where recreational metal detecting is permitted; check the land status map for the specific parcel before visiting.

locations.name: Pinos Altos Placer No.3
locations.description: A traveling assayer's offhand remark about the silver content of rocks near a mountain spring set off a staking rush in 1893 that placed the Pinos Altos Placer No.3 at its center. The ore ran high in silver with notable lead credits, and the smelter's payment schedule for lead kept the mine solvent during periods when silver prices weakened. Company housing lined a terrace above the mill; the rows of identical frame houses, company store, and assay office gave the camp the look of a planned industrial town. The mine's fatality rate was higher than average in its early years, reflecting both the dangerous ore body and management's tendency to prioritize production over safety improvements. An unusually rich ore chimney was discovered when development drifts on two adjacent levels connected unexpectedly through the ore body itself, revealing a vertical pipe of native metal that astonished even veterans. The property fell in the comfortable middle of the district's size spectrum — large enough to justify professional management and a proper mill, small enough to remain nimble during price downturns. Interpretive signs at the trailhead describe the mine's history for visitors exploring the surrounding public land; the actual mine site is a short walk from the parking area. Metal detecting is permitted on the BLM sections of this property; the New Mexico State Land Office manages adjacent parcels under different rules, so confirm the boundary before detecting.

locations.name: Pinos Altos Belle
locations.description: The Pinos Altos Belle took shape in 1860 when a systematic sampling program confirmed that the silver deposit extended far enough at depth to justify a full underground development program. Ruby silver minerals — pyrargyrite and proustite — appeared in the richest ore shoots and were sometimes preserved as specimens rather than sent to the smelter. The property changed hands twice during its productive years, each transfer bringing fresh capital and a new management approach that extended productive life beyond what the original operators had envisioned. The mine's ore reserve was carefully blocked out level by level; the systematic approach — unusual for its era — allowed management to plan production and investment on a multi-year horizon. A two-room schoolhouse built by the mining company's wives' auxiliary educated the district's children through all twelve grades at its peak enrollment. A spectacular ore pocket discovered on the seventh level — massive, almost pure native metal — funded a complete renovation of the mill and hoist plant that set the mine up for another decade of profitable operation. A well-organized medium-scale operation, the mine maintained consistent production over a long period rather than the dramatic boom-and-bust cycles that characterized many neighboring properties. Today the old mine yard is quiet, the headframe long since dismantled for salvage, but the outline of the ore bins and the concrete footings of the hoist room are still legible to a practiced eye. Metal detecting is permitted on the BLM sections of this property; the New Mexico State Land Office manages adjacent parcels under different rules, so confirm the boundary before detecting.

locations.name: Pinos Altos Chief
locations.description: The Pinos Altos Chief opened in 1863 on copper-bearing skarn contact rocks that miners had to tunnel through cold limestone before reaching the ore zone proper. The copper ore body was a classic porphyry-type deposit: large, low-grade, and requiring open-pit or large-scale underground block-caving methods to extract economically. Management installed a narrow-gauge tramway from the portal to the mill, replacing the mule-drawn ore wagons that had struggled over the rough canyon road. Ventilation was improved each time the mine deepened, with raise connections linking levels and powered fans installed at the portal to maintain a steady air circulation through the working stopes. Fourth of July celebrations at the mine drew miners from a fifty-mile radius, the day marked by drilling competitions, mule races, and the communal roasting of beef over wood fires. Two parallel veins converged at depth into a single rich structure, a geological geometry that the mine's resident engineer had predicted on theoretical grounds but proved only after years of systematic development. At peak operation the mine employed around sixty men and ran a ten-stamp mill that processed ore seven days a week during the productive seasons. The old camp site and mine workings have slowly been absorbed into the surrounding landscape, but careful observers can still find the collapsed ruins of the ore bins and the distinctive gray-white tailings. Metal detecting is generally allowed on the BLM-administered portions of this New Mexico district under casual-use provisions — verify the parcel boundary before heading out.

locations.description: Malachite and azurite staining on a hillside cliff band caught a geologist's eye in 1900, leading directly to the founding of the Pinos Altos Eagle and the opening of a new copper-mining chapter in the region. locations.name: Pinos Altos Fraction
locations.description: Silver-lead float scattered across the hillside in 1878 told experienced prospectors exactly where to sink the first shaft of the Pinos Altos Fraction. Ruby silver minerals — pyrargyrite and proustite — appeared in the richest ore shoots and were sometimes preserved as specimens rather than sent to the smelter. The company's annual reports to shareholders — several of which survive in state archives — provide unusually detailed production and cost data for a mine of this size and era. Shifts ran around the clock during peak years, the mine never sleeping as the demand for ore outpaced the mill's capacity to process it. A small town grew up around the mine: a post office, a two-room schoolhouse, three saloons, and the inevitable assay office that doubled as banker and source of local credit. A cave of the hanging wall at the deepest level sent shock waves through the entire underground workings and brought men scrambling from every portal, but miraculously caused no fatalities. The mine grew steadily through its first decade, reaching a workforce of nearly a hundred men and a ten-head stamp mill capable of treating several hundred tons of ore per month. A concrete collar caps the main shaft, and warning signs mark the perimeter of the historic workings, but visitors are free to walk the surrounding grounds and examine the extensive tailings. Much of this district falls under BLM jurisdiction where recreational metal detecting is permitted; check the land status map for the specific parcel before visiting.
