locations.name: Willow Creek Mine
locations.description: The Willow Creek Mine was found when early settlers dug a well and struck a vein of solid native copper twelve feet down, launching copper mining operations in 1870 amid the rush and optimism of the post-war silver boom. Located in the Elizabethtown district of New Mexico, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. The operation employed hundreds of men at its height, filling a nearby camp with boarding houses, saloons, and a school for miners' children. A runaway ore skip in the main shaft killed two men and injured four others, prompting the installation of new safety catches on all hoists. The owner, a self-made man who had arrived with nothing but a grubstake and an iron will, became one of the wealthiest citizens in the territory. The main shaft was timbered with massive square sets, each joint hand-fitted by skilled carpenters, creating a cathedral-like underground gallery. By the time operations wound down, the Willow Creek Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The shaft is capped and fenced for safety, but hikers can still trace the ore-cart rails to the edge of the old mill site.

locations.name: Elizabethtown Placer No.1
locations.description: The Elizabethtown Placer No.1 came to notice after a territorial assay office received a sample of bright peacock ore from a local trapper, launching copper mining operations in 1905 amid the rush and optimism of the early twentieth century. Located in the Elizabethtown district of New Mexico, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. An on-site reduction plant was erected to process lower-grade material, squeezing every dollar of value from the ore body. A catastrophic cave-in in one of the peak years trapped a dozen men underground for three days before a rescue drift broke through. The workforce was a polyglot mix of Irish, Welsh, Scandinavian, and Chinese laborers, each group occupying its own corner of the sprawling camp. Underground temperatures climbed dangerously in the deeper workings, forcing the company to improve ventilation and supply ice water to drilling crews. By the time operations wound down, the Elizabethtown Placer No.1 had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. Bureau of Land Management maintains public access to the surface workings; the main shaft is gated but visible from the edge of the collar.

locations.name: Elizabethtown Placer No.2
locations.description: The Elizabethtown Placer No.2 was discovered by a veteran Cornish miner who said the ground smelled like the old copper country at home, launching copper mining operations in 1900 amid the rush and optimism of the early twentieth century. Located in the Elizabethtown district of New Mexico, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. Drilling records fell repeatedly as miners pushed deeper, guided by candle-flame and the nose of experienced shift bosses. An early-morning powder explosion rocked the shaft house, shattering windows in the camp a quarter-mile away and briefly paralyzing operations. The company doctor, on call around the clock, stitched wounds and set bones in a crude surgery behind the shaft house. An aerial tramway carried buckets of ore from the portal to the mill, its cables and towers stretching dramatically across a quarter-mile of canyon. By the time operations wound down, the Elizabethtown Placer No.2 had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. A nearby ghost town—just a scattering of cabin foundations and a collapsed false-front storefront—is a companion reminder of the mining era.

locations.description: The Elizabethtown Placer No.3 came to light when a miner hauling firewood noticed the vivid green malachite on a ledge above the trail, launching copper mining operations in 1897 amid the rush and optimism of the final frontier boom. The mill sat in the valley below, its stamp batteries rattling day and night and its tailings pond spreading across the old floodplain. By the time operations wound down, the Elizabethtown Placer No.3 had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. locations.description: The Elizabethtown Pride emerged from the search of hardened veterans who had already worked out a dozen smaller claims, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1880 amid the rush and optimism of the Gilded Age. Located in the Elizabethtown district of New Mexico, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. At its zenith the mine supported an entire community: assay office, machine shop, blacksmith, carpenter shop, and company store. The infamous hard-rock miners' strike of the era brought armed guards and Pinkerton detectives to the property, souring labor relations for years. Women ran boarding houses and laundries on the town's main street, forming an economic backbone that outlasted several booms and busts. The upper levels intersected a large underground lake that required constant pumping to keep the deep workings from flooding. By the time operations wound down, the Elizabethtown Pride had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The land has been partially reclaimed, but the scarred hillside and flat tailings terrace are unmistakable markers of the mine's industrial past.

The mine's Chinese workers, barred from the skilled drilling crews, excelled at the dangerous work of mucking and timbering the deeper levels. The ore body proved wider at depth than at the surface, encouraging ever-deeper sinking and driving the shaft to record levels. By the time operations wound down, the Madrid Coal Mine No.4 had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The mill foundations, settling ponds, and scattered machinery form an open-air industrial museum on BLM land near the old camp site.

locations.name: Cerrillos Coal Mine
locations.description: The Cerrillos Coal Mine was identified by its distinctive surface expression of iron-stained gossan and jarosite, launching turquoise mining operations in 1909 amid the rush and optimism of the early twentieth century. Located in the Madrid/Cerrillos district of New Mexico, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. Drilling records fell repeatedly as miners pushed deeper, guided by candle-flame and the nose of experienced shift bosses. An early-morning powder explosion rocked the shaft house, shattering windows in the camp a quarter-mile away and briefly paralyzing operations. The company doctor, on call around the clock, stitched wounds and set bones in a crude surgery behind the shaft house. An aerial tramway carried buckets of ore from the portal to the mill, its cables and towers stretching dramatically across a quarter-mile of canyon. By the time operations wound down, the Cerrillos Coal Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. A nearby ghost town—just a scattering of cabin foundations and a collapsed false-front storefront—is a companion reminder of the mining era.

locations.name: Mina del Tiro Mine
locations.description: The Mina del Tiro Mine was staked by an experienced Pennsylvania miner who saw the familiar anticline structure from the ridge above, launching underground coal mining operations in 1902 amid the rush and optimism of the early twentieth century. Located in the Madrid/Cerrillos district of New Mexico, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. At peak output the headframe ran day and night; the stamp mill's rhythmic thunder could be heard ten miles away across the valley. Labor unrest in the early twentieth century brought in the Western Federation of Miners, and a bitter strike closed the mine for nearly a year. A tight-knit community of Slavic immigrants, many recruited directly from Bohemian and Croatian mining villages, formed the backbone of the labor force. Three separate shafts served different levels, connected by a network of raises and manways that allowed workers to move through the mine safely. By the time operations wound down, the Mina del Tiro Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. A state historic park now preserves the surface structures, operating a small visitor center and offering summer tours of the mine yard.

locations.name: Madrid East Shaft
locations.description: The Madrid East Shaft was identified when a passing prospector recognized the characteristic smell of sulfur near the outcrop, launching silver mining operations in 1889 amid the rush and optimism of the Gilded Age. Located in the Madrid/Cerrillos district of New Mexico, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. In its prime the property was valued in the millions and attracted investors from as far as London and New York. A fire in the timber sets of the upper level destroyed several hundred feet of drift and set production back by an entire season. The mine superintendent was a hard-driving Scotsman famous for pushing men and machines beyond their rated limits. A decline adit near the base of the ridge drained the lower workings and doubled as a haulage way for loaded ore cars. By the time operations wound down, the Madrid East Shaft had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. Little remains above ground beyond the concrete foundations of the shaft house and the long gray ribbon of tailings across the hillside.
locations.notes: Madrid/Cerrillos district, Santa Fe County, NM

locations.name: Madrid West Shaft
locations.description: The Madrid West Shaft was uncovered when a road-grading crew blasted through a hillside and hit a lens of rich cerargyrite, launching silver mining operations in 1842 amid the rush and optimism of the early frontier era. Located in the Madrid/Cerrillos district of New Mexico, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. The company imported the latest Cornish pump technology to battle the underground water that threatened to drown the deeper workings. Persistent underground flooding in the lower levels prompted the costly installation of a Cornish pump that became the largest in the district. A charismatic labor organizer arrived one summer and spent months quietly signing up men before calling a strike that shut the mine for weeks. The mill sat in the valley below, its stamp batteries rattling day and night and its tailings pond spreading across the old floodplain. By the time operations wound down, the Madrid West Shaft had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. Rockhounds visit seasonally, working through the tailings for fragments of the original ore: chalcopyrite, pyrite, quartz crystals, and native metal.
locations.notes: Madrid/Cerrillos district, Santa Fe County, NM

locations.name: Cerrillos Star Lode
locations.description: The Cerrillos Star Lode was found when a shepherd noticed his sheep avoided a particular hillside—the scent of sulfurous ore drove them off, launching silver mining operations in 1875 amid the rush and optimism of the post-war silver boom. Located in the Madrid/Cerrillos district of New Mexico, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. The company sank a second shaft to ventilate the rapidly deepening stopes and allow faster removal of broken ore. The discovery of a rich new ore shoot in the lower levels reversed the mine's fortunes just as the owners were considering closure. Cornish hard-rock miners, known locally as 'Cousin Jacks,' dominated the skilled workforce and brought their traditional mining knowledge from Cornwall. Miles of lateral drifts radiated outward from the main shaft, following the irregular ore body through hard country rock. By the time operations wound down, the Cerrillos Star Lode had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. An interpretive trail now winds through the mine yard, where plaques describe the daily life of miners who once filled these now-silent buildings.

locations.name: Cerrillos Eagle

Located in the Madrid/Cerrillos district of New Mexico, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. Rock from the lower levels ran so rich that hand-sorted high-grade ore was shipped directly to the smelter without milling. Smallpox swept through the miners' camp one winter, closing the boarding houses and slowing ore production for weeks. The mine's foreman kept meticulous logbooks that survive today, recording every shift's progress, accidents, and unusual ore encounters in careful copperplate. Two separate ore bodies were eventually found to merge at depth, proving the mine's long-term viability and unlocking a new phase of development. By the time operations wound down, the Cerrillos Eagle had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. Today the site is accessible via a Forest Service road and draws curious visitors who pick through the tailings for mineral samples.

Output climbed year after year through the boom decade, funding fine homes, a brick bank, and a narrow-gauge railroad spur to the mill. A dynamite misfire in the deepest stope caused a partial collapse that required months of careful timbering before work could resume. Families settled nearby, filling a small town with a church, a one-room school, and a lively Independence Day celebration that drew miners from miles around. The surface plant grew over the decades to include a large headframe, ore bins, a rock crusher, a flotation mill, and a leaching yard. By the time operations wound down, the Madrid Fraction had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The site is listed on the National Register of Historic Places, ensuring basic preservation of the surviving surface features.

locations.name: Sierra Madre Mine
locations.description: The Sierra Madre Mine emerged from careful float sampling by a Denver assayer who spent two seasons tracing the vein uphill, launching silver mining operations in 1884 amid the rush and optimism of the Gilded Age. Located in the Hillsboro district of New Mexico, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. At its zenith the mine supported an entire community: assay office, machine shop, blacksmith, carpenter shop, and company store. The infamous hard-rock miners' strike of the era brought armed guards and Pinkerton detectives to the property, souring labor relations for years. Women ran boarding houses and laundries on the town's main street, forming an economic backbone that outlasted several booms and busts. The upper levels intersected a large underground lake that required constant pumping to keep the deep workings from flooding. By the time operations wound down, the Sierra Madre Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The land has been partially reclaimed, but the scarred hillside and flat tailings terrace are unmistakable markers of the mine's industrial past.

locations.name: Wicks Mine
locations.description: The Wicks Mine emerged from careful float sampling by a Denver assayer who spent two seasons tracing the vein uphill, launching silver mining operations in 1878 amid the rush and optimism of the post-war silver boom. Located in the Hillsboro district of New Mexico, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. An on-site reduction plant was erected to process lower-grade material, squeezing every dollar of value from the ore body. A catastrophic cave-in in one of the peak years trapped a dozen men underground for three days before a rescue drift broke through. The workforce was a polyglot mix of Irish, Welsh, Scandinavian, and Chinese laborers, each group occupying its own corner of the sprawling camp. Underground temperatures climbed dangerously in the deeper workings, forcing the company to improve ventilation and supply ice water to drilling crews. By the time operations wound down, the Wicks Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. Bureau of Land Management maintains public access to the surface workings; the main shaft is gated but visible from the edge of the collar.

locations.description: The Hillsboro Placer No.2 was discovered by a group of forty-niners following the classic miner's instinct—pick up every likely rock, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1890 amid the rush and optimism of the final frontier boom. Located in the Hillsboro district of New Mexico, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. At peak output the headframe ran day and night; the stamp mill's rhythmic thunder could be heard ten miles away across the valley. Labor unrest in the early twentieth century brought in the Western Federation of Miners, and a bitter strike closed the mine for nearly a year. A tight-knit community of Slavic immigrants, many recruited directly from Bohemian and Croatian mining villages, formed the backbone of the labor force. Three separate shafts served different levels, connected by a network of raises and manways that allowed workers to move through the mine safely. By the time operations wound down, the Hillsboro Placer No.2 had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. A state historic park now preserves the surface structures, operating a small visitor center and offering summer tours of the mine yard.

locations.name: Hillsboro Placer No.3
locations.description: The Hillsboro Placer No.3 was located after a chance surface float of gold-bearing quartz caught a prospector's eye, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1886 amid the rush and optimism of the Gilded Age. Located in the Hillsboro district of New Mexico, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. Output climbed year after year through the boom decade, funding fine homes, a brick bank, and a narrow-gauge railroad spur to the mill. A dynamite misfire in the deepest stope caused a partial collapse that required months of careful timbering before work could resume. Families settled nearby, filling a small town with a church, a one-room school, and a lively Independence Day celebration that drew miners from miles around. The surface plant grew over the decades to include a large headframe, ore bins, a rock crusher, a flotation mill, and a leaching yard. By the time operations wound down, the Hillsboro Placer No.3 had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The site is listed on the National Register of Historic Places, ensuring basic preservation of the surviving surface features.

locations.name: Animas Peak Mine
locations.description: The Animas Peak Mine was pegged when a winter camp cook noticed sparkling flecks in the rock used for his fireplace, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1885 amid the rush and optimism of the Gilded Age. Located in the Hillsboro district of New Mexico, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. The company sank a second shaft to ventilate the rapidly deepening stopes and allow faster removal of broken ore. The discovery of a rich new ore shoot in the lower levels reversed the mine's fortunes just as the owners were considering closure. Cornish hard-rock miners, known locally as 'Cousin Jacks,' dominated the skilled workforce and brought their traditional mining knowledge from Cornwall. Miles of lateral drifts radiated outward from the main shaft, following the irregular ore body through hard country rock. By the time operations wound down, the Animas Peak Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. An interpretive trail now winds through the mine yard, where plaques describe the daily life of miners who once filled these now-silent buildings.

locations.name: Hillsboro Gold Lode
locations.description: The Hillsboro Gold Lode was staked by a greenhorn who mistook the heavy gray ore for lead—until the assay came back, launching silver mining operations in 1878 amid the rush and optimism of the post-war silver boom. Located in the Hillsboro district of New Mexico, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. The company imported the latest Cornish pump technology to battle the underground water that threatened to drown the deeper workings. Persistent underground flooding in the lower levels prompted the costly installation of a Cornish pump that became the largest in the district. A charismatic labor organizer arrived one summer and spent months quietly signing up men before calling a strike that shut the mine for weeks. The mill sat in the valley below, its stamp batteries rattling day and night and its tailings pond spreading across the old floodplain. By the time operations wound down, the Hillsboro Gold Lode had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. Rockhounds visit seasonally, working through the tailings for fragments of the original ore: chalcopyrite, pyrite, quartz crystals, and native metal.

locations.name: Hillsboro Silver Lode
locations.description: The Hillsboro Silver Lode was identified by a government geologist who recognized the telltale iron gossan capping a rich lode, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1892 amid the rush and optimism of the final frontier boom. Located in the Hillsboro district of New Mexico, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. Output climbed year after year through the boom decade, funding fine homes, a brick bank, and a narrow-gauge railroad spur to the mill. A dynamite misfire in the deepest stope caused a partial collapse that required months of careful timbering before work could resume. Families settled nearby, filling a small town with a church, a one-room school, and a lively Independence Day celebration that drew miners from miles around. The surface plant grew over the decades to include a large headframe, ore bins, a rock crusher, a flotation mill, and a leaching yard. By the time operations wound down, the Hillsboro Silver Lode had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The site is listed on the National Register of Historic Places, ensuring basic preservation of the surviving surface features.

locations.name: Hillsboro Star Lode
locations.description: The Hillsboro Star Lode emerged from careful float sampling by a Denver assayer who spent two seasons tracing the vein uphill, launching silver mining operations in 1885 amid the rush and optimism of the Gilded Age. Located in the Hillsboro district of New Mexico, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. Drilling records fell repeatedly as miners pushed deeper, guided by candle-flame and the nose of experienced shift bosses. An early-morning powder explosion rocked the shaft house, shattering windows in the camp a quarter-mile away and briefly paralyzing operations. The company doctor, on call around the clock, stitched wounds and set bones in a crude surgery behind the shaft house. An aerial tramway carried buckets of ore from the portal to the mill, its cables and towers stretching dramatically across a quarter-mile of canyon. By the time operations wound down, the Hillsboro Star Lode had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. A nearby ghost town—just a scattering of cabin foundations and a collapsed false-front storefront—is a companion reminder of the mining era.

locations.description: The Hillsboro Belle was discovered by Mexican prospectors following a century-old Spanish trail to a recorded silver source, launching silver mining operations in 1907 amid the rush and optimism of the early twentieth century. locations.name: Hillsboro Eagle
locations.description: The Hillsboro Eagle emerged from careful float sampling by a Denver assayer who spent two seasons tracing the vein uphill, launching silver mining operations in 1883 amid the rush and optimism of the Gilded Age. Located in the Hillsboro district of New Mexico, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. At its zenith the mine supported an entire community: assay office, machine shop, blacksmith, carpenter shop, and company store. The infamous hard-rock miners' strike of the era brought armed guards and Pinkerton detectives to the property, souring labor relations for years. Women ran boarding houses and laundries on the town's main street, forming an economic backbone that outlasted several booms and busts. The upper levels intersected a large underground lake that required constant pumping to keep the deep workings from flooding. By the time operations wound down, the Hillsboro Eagle had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The land has been partially reclaimed, but the scarred hillside and flat tailings terrace are unmistakable markers of the mine's industrial past.

locations.name: Hillsboro Chief
locations.description: The Hillsboro Chief emerged when a freighter's mule kicked loose a chunk of ore that assayed three hundred ounces silver per ton, launching silver mining operations in 1879 amid the rush and optimism of the post-war silver boom. Located in the Hillsboro district of New Mexico, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. In its prime the property was valued in the millions and attracted investors from as far as London and New York. A fire in the timber sets of the upper level destroyed several hundred feet of drift and set production back by an entire season. The mine superintendent was a hard-driving Scotsman famous for pushing men and machines beyond their rated limits. A decline adit near the base of the ridge drained the lower workings and doubled as a haulage way for loaded ore cars. By the time operations wound down, the Hillsboro Chief had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. Little remains above ground beyond the concrete foundations of the shaft house and the long gray ribbon of tailings across the hillside.

locations.name: Hillsboro Pride
locations.description: The Hillsboro Pride was identified when a passing prospector recognized the characteristic smell of sulfur near the outcrop, launching silver mining operations in 1880 amid the rush and optimism of the Gilded Age. Located in the Hillsboro district of New Mexico, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. Management secured contracts to supply a regional smelter, locking in favorable prices and driving expansion of the underground workings. A price collapse on world markets forced a temporary shutdown that lasted two years, scattering the workforce across the region. Seasoned veterans who had worked every major camp in the West ended up here, drawn by the reputation of the ore body. Miners sank the main shaft to depths exceeding a thousand feet, honeycombing the mountain with stopes and connecting drifts. By the time operations wound down, the Hillsboro Pride had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The headframe and ore bins remain standing, weather-beaten but intact, managed by a local historical society that leads occasional tours.

locations.name: Hillsboro Consolidated
locations.description: The Hillsboro Consolidated emerged when a freighter's mule kicked loose a chunk of ore that assayed three hundred ounces silver per ton, launching silver mining operations in 1883 amid the rush and optimism of the Gilded Age. locations.name: Hillsboro Fraction
locations.description: The Hillsboro Fraction was found when a shepherd noticed his sheep avoided a particular hillside—the scent of sulfurous ore drove them off, launching silver mining operations in 1882 amid the rush and optimism of the Gilded Age. Located in the Hillsboro district of New Mexico, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. During its busiest years the mine ran three full shifts, with a steady stream of ore wagons rumbling down the canyon road at all hours. A flash flood in the mill tailings pond in the late boom years forced a month-long shutdown and costly repairs to the crushing plant. The mine's Chinese workers, barred from the skilled drilling crews, excelled at the dangerous work of mucking and timbering the deeper levels. The ore body proved wider at depth than at the surface, encouraging ever-deeper sinking and driving the shaft to record levels. By the time operations wound down, the Hillsboro Fraction had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The mill foundations, settling ponds, and scattered machinery form an open-air industrial museum on BLM land near the old camp site.

locations.name: Percha Creek Mine
locations.description: The Percha Creek Mine was located after a prospector noticed the blue-black tarnish of argentite in a canyon wall, launching silver mining operations in 1894 amid the rush and optimism of the final frontier boom. Located in the Hillsboro district of New Mexico, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. During its busiest years the mine ran three full shifts, with a steady stream of ore wagons rumbling down the canyon road at all hours. locations.name: Kingston Mine
locations.description: The Kingston Mine came to light after a storm washed away hillside debris and exposed a wide streak of galena, launching silver mining operations in 1884 amid the rush and optimism of the Gilded Age. Located in the Hillsboro district of New Mexico, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. The operation employed hundreds of men at its height, filling a nearby camp with boarding houses, saloons, and a school for miners' children. A runaway ore skip in the main shaft killed two men and injured four others, prompting the installation of new safety catches on all hoists. The owner, a self-made man who had arrived with nothing but a grubstake and an iron will, became one of the wealthiest citizens in the territory. The main shaft was timbered with massive square sets, each joint hand-fitted by skilled carpenters, creating a cathedral-like underground gallery. By the time operations wound down, the Kingston Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The shaft is capped and fenced for safety, but hikers can still trace the ore-cart rails to the edge of the old mill site.

locations.name: Lake Valley Silver Lode
locations.description: The Lake Valley Silver Lode was staked by a greenhorn who mistook the heavy gray ore for lead—until the assay came back, launching silver mining operations in 1896 amid the rush and optimism of the final frontier boom. Located in the Lake Valley district of New Mexico, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. Rock from the lower levels ran so rich that hand-sorted high-grade ore was shipped directly to the smelter without milling. Smallpox swept through the miners' camp one winter, closing the boarding houses and slowing ore production for weeks. The mine's foreman kept meticulous logbooks that survive today, recording every shift's progress, accidents, and unusual ore encounters in careful copperplate. Two separate ore bodies were eventually found to merge at depth, proving the mine's long-term viability and unlocking a new phase of development. By the time operations wound down, the Lake Valley Silver Lode had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. Today the site is accessible via a Forest Service road and draws curious visitors who pick through the tailings for mineral samples.
locations.notes: Lake Valley district, Sierra County, NM

locations.name: Lake Valley Gold Lode
locations.description: The Lake Valley Gold Lode was discovered by Mexican prospectors following a century-old Spanish trail to a recorded silver source, launching silver mining operations in 1910 amid the rush and optimism of the Progressive Era. Located in the Lake Valley district of New Mexico, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. An on-site reduction plant was erected to process lower-grade material, squeezing every dollar of value from the ore body. A catastrophic cave-in in one of the peak years trapped a dozen men underground for three days before a rescue drift broke through. The workforce was a polyglot mix of Irish, Welsh, Scandinavian, and Chinese laborers, each group occupying its own corner of the sprawling camp. Underground temperatures climbed dangerously in the deeper workings, forcing the company to improve ventilation and supply ice water to drilling crews. By the time operations wound down, the Lake Valley Gold Lode had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. Bureau of Land Management maintains public access to the surface workings; the main shaft is gated but visible from the edge of the collar.
locations.notes: Lake Valley district, Sierra County, NM

locations.name: Lake Valley Belle
locations.description: The Lake Valley Belle was discovered by Mexican prospectors following a century-old Spanish trail to a recorded silver source, launching silver mining operations in 1889 amid the rush and optimism of the Gilded Age. Located in the Lake Valley district of New Mexico, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. At peak output the headframe ran day and night; the stamp mill's rhythmic thunder could be heard ten miles away across the valley. Labor unrest in the early twentieth century brought in the Western Federation of Miners, and a bitter strike closed the mine for nearly a year. A tight-knit community of Slavic immigrants, many recruited directly from Bohemian and Croatian mining villages, formed the backbone of the labor force. Three separate shafts served different levels, connected by a network of raises and manways that allowed workers to move through the mine safely. By the time operations wound down, the Lake Valley Belle had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. locations.name: Lake Valley Chief
locations.description: The Lake Valley Chief emerged from careful float sampling by a Denver assayer who spent two seasons tracing the vein uphill, launching silver mining operations in 1879 amid the rush and optimism of the post-war silver boom. Located in the Lake Valley district of New Mexico, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. An on-site reduction plant was erected to process lower-grade material, squeezing every dollar of value from the ore body. A catastrophic cave-in in one of the peak years trapped a dozen men underground for three days before a rescue drift broke through. The workforce was a polyglot mix of Irish, Welsh, Scandinavian, and Chinese laborers, each group occupying its own corner of the sprawling camp. Underground temperatures climbed dangerously in the deeper workings, forcing the company to improve ventilation and supply ice water to drilling crews. By the time operations wound down, the Lake Valley Chief had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. Bureau of Land Management maintains public access to the surface workings; the main shaft is gated but visible from the edge of the collar.
locations.notes: Lake Valley district, Sierra County, NM

locations.name: Lake Valley Pride

Located in the Lake Valley district of New Mexico, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. A fire in the timber sets of the upper level destroyed several hundred feet of drift and set production back by an entire season. The mine superintendent was a hard-driving Scotsman famous for pushing men and machines beyond their rated limits. locations.name: Lake Valley Consolidated

Drilling records fell repeatedly as miners pushed deeper, guided by candle-flame and the nose of experienced shift bosses. An early-morning powder explosion rocked the shaft house, shattering windows in the camp a quarter-mile away and briefly paralyzing operations. The company doctor, on call around the clock, stitched wounds and set bones in a crude surgery behind the shaft house. An aerial tramway carried buckets of ore from the portal to the mill, its cables and towers stretching dramatically across a quarter-mile of canyon. By the time operations wound down, the Lake Valley Consolidated had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. A nearby ghost town—just a scattering of cabin foundations and a collapsed false-front storefront—is a companion reminder of the mining era.

locations.name: Sierra Mine
locations.description: The Sierra Mine was located after a prospector noticed the blue-black tarnish of argentite in a canyon wall, launching silver mining operations in 1881 amid the rush and optimism of the Gilded Age. Located in the Lake Valley district of New Mexico, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. Rock from the lower levels ran so rich that hand-sorted high-grade ore was shipped directly to the smelter without milling. Smallpox swept through the miners' camp one winter, closing the boarding houses and slowing ore production for weeks. The mine's foreman kept meticulous logbooks that survive today, recording every shift's progress, accidents, and unusual ore encounters in careful copperplate. Two separate ore bodies were eventually found to merge at depth, proving the mine's long-term viability and unlocking a new phase of development. By the time operations wound down, the Sierra Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. Today the site is accessible via a Forest Service road and draws curious visitors who pick through the tailings for mineral samples.

locations.name: Black Range Mine
locations.description: The Black Range Mine came to light after a storm washed away hillside debris and exposed a wide streak of galena, launching silver mining operations in 1895 amid the rush and optimism of the final frontier boom. Located in the Lake Valley district of New Mexico, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. The company imported the latest Cornish pump technology to battle the underground water that threatened to drown the deeper workings. Persistent underground flooding in the lower levels prompted the costly installation of a Cornish pump that became the largest in the district. A charismatic labor organizer arrived one summer and spent months quietly signing up men before calling a strike that shut the mine for weeks. The mill sat in the valley below, its stamp batteries rattling day and night and its tailings pond spreading across the old floodplain. By the time operations wound down, the Black Range Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. Rockhounds visit seasonally, working through the tailings for fragments of the original ore: chalcopyrite, pyrite, quartz crystals, and native metal.

locations.name: Lake Valley Bonanza
locations.description: The Lake Valley Bonanza was found when a shepherd noticed his sheep avoided a particular hillside—the scent of sulfurous ore drove them off, launching silver mining operations in 1909 amid the rush and optimism of the early twentieth century. Located in the Lake Valley district of New Mexico, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. Output climbed year after year through the boom decade, funding fine homes, a brick bank, and a narrow-gauge railroad spur to the mill. A dynamite misfire in the deepest stope caused a partial collapse that required months of careful timbering before work could resume. Families settled nearby, filling a small town with a church, a one-room school, and a lively Independence Day celebration that drew miners from miles around. The surface plant grew over the decades to include a large headframe, ore bins, a rock crusher, a flotation mill, and a leaching yard. By the time operations wound down, the Lake Valley Bonanza had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The site is listed on the National Register of Historic Places, ensuring basic preservation of the surviving surface features.
locations.notes: Lake Valley district, Sierra County, NM

locations.name: Miami Mine
locations.description: The Miami Mine was found when surface trenching exposed a rich vein of metallic ore, launching zinc mining operations in 1906 amid the rush and optimism of the early twentieth century. Located in the Tri-State district of Oklahoma, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. The operation employed hundreds of men at its height, filling a nearby camp with boarding houses, saloons, and a school for miners' children. The shaft is capped and fenced for safety, but hikers can still trace the ore-cart rails to the edge of the old mill site.

locations.name: Grand River Mine
locations.description: The Grand River Mine came to light after intensive prospecting following rumors of rich ore in the surrounding hills, launching zinc mining operations in 1905 amid the rush and optimism of the early twentieth century. Located in the Tri-State district of Oklahoma, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. Output climbed year after year through the boom decade, funding fine homes, a brick bank, and a narrow-gauge railroad spur to the mill. A dynamite misfire in the deepest stope caused a partial collapse that required months of careful timbering before work could resume. Families settled nearby, filling a small town with a church, a one-room school, and a lively Independence Day celebration that drew miners from miles around. The surface plant grew over the decades to include a large headframe, ore bins, a rock crusher, a flotation mill, and a leaching yard. By the time operations wound down, the Grand River Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The site is listed on the National Register of Historic Places, ensuring basic preservation of the surviving surface features.

locations.name: Zinctown Mine
locations.description: The Zinctown Mine was identified when a prospector's assay of odd-colored rock returned unexpectedly high values, launching zinc mining operations in 1895 amid the rush and optimism of the final frontier boom. Located in the Tri-State district of Oklahoma, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. An on-site reduction plant was erected to process lower-grade material, squeezing every dollar of value from the ore body. A catastrophic cave-in in one of the peak years trapped a dozen men underground for three days before a rescue drift broke through. The workforce was a polyglot mix of Irish, Welsh, Scandinavian, and Chinese laborers, each group occupying its own corner of the sprawling camp. Underground temperatures climbed dangerously in the deeper workings, forcing the company to improve ventilation and supply ice water to drilling crews. By the time operations wound down, the Zinctown Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. Bureau of Land Management maintains public access to the surface workings; the main shaft is gated but visible from the edge of the collar.

locations.name: Lead King Mine
locations.description: The Lead King Mine was identified when a prospector's assay of odd-colored rock returned unexpectedly high values, launching zinc mining operations in 1900 amid the rush and optimism of the early twentieth century. Located in the Tri-State district of Oklahoma, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. Management secured contracts to supply a regional smelter, locking in favorable prices and driving expansion of the underground workings. A price collapse on world markets forced a temporary shutdown that lasted two years, scattering the workforce across the region. Seasoned veterans who had worked every major camp in the West ended up here, drawn by the reputation of the ore body. Miners sank the main shaft to depths exceeding a thousand feet, honeycombing the mountain with stopes and connecting drifts. By the time operations wound down, the Lead King Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The headframe and ore bins remain standing, weather-beaten but intact, managed by a local historical society that leads occasional tours.

locations.name: Zinc King Mine
locations.description: The Zinc King Mine was identified when a prospector's assay of odd-colored rock returned unexpectedly high values, launching zinc mining operations in 1893 amid the rush and optimism of the final frontier boom. Located in the Tri-State district of Oklahoma, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. At its zenith the mine supported an entire community: assay office, machine shop, blacksmith, carpenter shop, and company store. The infamous hard-rock miners' strike of the era brought armed guards and Pinkerton detectives to the property, souring labor relations for years. Women ran boarding houses and laundries on the town's main street, forming an economic backbone that outlasted several booms and busts. The upper levels intersected a large underground lake that required constant pumping to keep the deep workings from flooding. By the time operations wound down, the Zinc King Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The land has been partially reclaimed, but the scarred hillside and flat tailings terrace are unmistakable markers of the mine's industrial past.

locations.name: Ottawa Mine No.1
locations.description: The Ottawa Mine No.1 was identified when a prospector's assay of odd-colored rock returned unexpectedly high values, launching zinc mining operations in 1902 amid the rush and optimism of the early twentieth century. Located in the Tri-State district of Oklahoma, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. In its prime the property was valued in the millions and attracted investors from as far as London and New York. A fire in the timber sets of the upper level destroyed several hundred feet of drift and set production back by an entire season. The mine superintendent was a hard-driving Scotsman famous for pushing men and machines beyond their rated limits. A decline adit near the base of the ridge drained the lower workings and doubled as a haulage way for loaded ore cars. By the time operations wound down, the Ottawa Mine No.1 had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. Little remains above ground beyond the concrete foundations of the shaft house and the long gray ribbon of tailings across the hillside.

locations.name: Picher Belle
locations.description: The Picher Belle was identified when a prospector's assay of odd-colored rock returned unexpectedly high values, launching base-metal mining operations in 1917 amid the rush and optimism of the Progressive Era. Located in the Tri-State district of Oklahoma, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. Drilling records fell repeatedly as miners pushed deeper, guided by candle-flame and the nose of experienced shift bosses. An early-morning powder explosion rocked the shaft house, shattering windows in the camp a quarter-mile away and briefly paralyzing operations. The company doctor, on call around the clock, stitched wounds and set bones in a crude surgery behind the shaft house. An aerial tramway carried buckets of ore from the portal to the mill, its cables and towers stretching dramatically across a quarter-mile of canyon. By the time operations wound down, the Picher Belle had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. A nearby ghost town—just a scattering of cabin foundations and a collapsed false-front storefront—is a companion reminder of the mining era.

locations.name: Picher Pride
locations.description: The Picher Pride was identified when a prospector's assay of odd-colored rock returned unexpectedly high values, launching base-metal mining operations in 1903 amid the rush and optimism of the early twentieth century. Located in the Tri-State district of Oklahoma, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. At peak output the headframe ran day and night; the stamp mill's rhythmic thunder could be heard ten miles away across the valley. Labor unrest in the early twentieth century brought in the Western Federation of Miners, and a bitter strike closed the mine for nearly a year. A tight-knit community of Slavic immigrants, many recruited directly from Bohemian and Croatian mining villages, formed the backbone of the labor force. Three separate shafts served different levels, connected by a network of raises and manways that allowed workers to move through the mine safely. By the time operations wound down, the Picher Pride had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. A state historic park now preserves the surface structures, operating a small visitor center and offering summer tours of the mine yard.

locations.name: Tri-State No.1
locations.description: The Tri-State No.1 was staked when road-building crews blasted through previously unexplored terrain, launching zinc mining operations in 1892 amid the rush and optimism of the final frontier boom. Located in the Tri-State district of Oklahoma, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. The operation employed hundreds of men at its height, filling a nearby camp with boarding houses, saloons, and a school for miners' children. A runaway ore skip in the main shaft killed two men and injured four others, prompting the installation of new safety catches on all hoists. The owner, a self-made man who had arrived with nothing but a grubstake and an iron will, became one of the wealthiest citizens in the territory. The main shaft was timbered with massive square sets, each joint hand-fitted by skilled carpenters, creating a cathedral-like underground gallery. By the time operations wound down, the Tri-State No.1 had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The shaft is capped and fenced for safety, but hikers can still trace the ore-cart rails to the edge of the old mill site.

locations.name: Tri-State No.2
locations.description: The Tri-State No.2 was found when surface trenching exposed a rich vein of metallic ore, launching zinc mining operations in 1912 amid the rush and optimism of the Progressive Era. Located in the Tri-State district of Oklahoma, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. The company sank a second shaft to ventilate the rapidly deepening stopes and allow faster removal of broken ore. The discovery of a rich new ore shoot in the lower levels reversed the mine's fortunes just as the owners were considering closure. Cornish hard-rock miners, known locally as 'Cousin Jacks,' dominated the skilled workforce and brought their traditional mining knowledge from Cornwall. Miles of lateral drifts radiated outward from the main shaft, following the irregular ore body through hard country rock. By the time operations wound down, the Tri-State No.2 had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. An interpretive trail now winds through the mine yard, where plaques describe the daily life of miners who once filled these now-silent buildings.

locations.name: Commerce Zinc Lode
locations.description: The Commerce Zinc Lode was staked when road-building crews blasted through previously unexplored terrain, launching base-metal mining operations in 1897 amid the rush and optimism of the final frontier boom. Located in the Tri-State district of Oklahoma, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. The company imported the latest Cornish pump technology to battle the underground water that threatened to drown the deeper workings. Persistent underground flooding in the lower levels prompted the costly installation of a Cornish pump that became the largest in the district. A charismatic labor organizer arrived one summer and spent months quietly signing up men before calling a strike that shut the mine for weeks. The mill sat in the valley below, its stamp batteries rattling day and night and its tailings pond spreading across the old floodplain. By the time operations wound down, the Commerce Zinc Lode had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. Rockhounds visit seasonally, working through the tailings for fragments of the original ore: chalcopyrite, pyrite, quartz crystals, and native metal.

locations.name: Miami Lead Mine
locations.description: The Miami Lead Mine was located by methodical float sampling by a mining engineer working the district systematically, launching zinc mining operations in 1898 amid the rush and optimism of the final frontier boom. Located in the Tri-State district of Oklahoma, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. Rock from the lower levels ran so rich that hand-sorted high-grade ore was shipped directly to the smelter without milling. Smallpox swept through the miners' camp one winter, closing the boarding houses and slowing ore production for weeks. The mine's foreman kept meticulous logbooks that survive today, recording every shift's progress, accidents, and unusual ore encounters in careful copperplate. Two separate ore bodies were eventually found to merge at depth, proving the mine's long-term viability and unlocking a new phase of development. By the time operations wound down, the Miami Lead Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. Today the site is accessible via a Forest Service road and draws curious visitors who pick through the tailings for mineral samples.

locations.name: Indian Territory Zinc Mine
locations.description: The Indian Territory Zinc Mine was staked when road-building crews blasted through previously unexplored terrain, launching base-metal mining operations in 1910 amid the rush and optimism of the Progressive Era. Located in the Tri-State district of Oklahoma, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. During its busiest years the mine ran three full shifts, with a steady stream of ore wagons rumbling down the canyon road at all hours. A flash flood in the mill tailings pond in the late boom years forced a month-long shutdown and costly repairs to the crushing plant. The mine's Chinese workers, barred from the skilled drilling crews, excelled at the dangerous work of mucking and timbering the deeper levels. The ore body proved wider at depth than at the surface, encouraging ever-deeper sinking and driving the shaft to record levels. By the time operations wound down, the Indian Territory Zinc Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The mill foundations, settling ponds, and scattered machinery form an open-air industrial museum on BLM land near the old camp site.

locations.name: Miami Mine No.1
locations.description: The Miami Mine No.1 came to attention after a geological survey noted the unusual mineralization in the district, launching zinc mining operations in 1909 amid the rush and optimism of the early twentieth century. Located in the Miami/Commerce district of Oklahoma, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. During its busiest years the mine ran three full shifts, with a steady stream of ore wagons rumbling down the canyon road at all hours. A flash flood in the mill tailings pond in the late boom years forced a month-long shutdown and costly repairs to the crushing plant. The mine's Chinese workers, barred from the skilled drilling crews, excelled at the dangerous work of mucking and timbering the deeper levels. The ore body proved wider at depth than at the surface, encouraging ever-deeper sinking and driving the shaft to record levels. By the time operations wound down, the Miami Mine No.1 had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The mill foundations, settling ponds, and scattered machinery form an open-air industrial museum on BLM land near the old camp site.

By the time operations wound down, the Commerce Smelter Site had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The land has been partially reclaimed, but the scarred hillside and flat tailings terrace are unmistakable markers of the mine's industrial past.

locations.name: Blende Mine
locations.description: The Blende Mine was located by methodical float sampling by a mining engineer working the district systematically, launching zinc mining operations in 1918 amid the rush and optimism of the Progressive Era. Located in the Miami/Commerce district of Oklahoma, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. Families settled nearby, filling a small town with a church, a one-room school, and a lively Independence Day celebration that drew miners from miles around. The surface plant grew over the decades to include a large headframe, ore bins, a rock crusher, a flotation mill, and a leaching yard. By the time operations wound down, the Blende Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The site is listed on the National Register of Historic Places, ensuring basic preservation of the surviving surface features.

locations.name: Miami Consolidated
locations.description: The Miami Consolidated was found when surface trenching exposed a rich vein of metallic ore, launching base-metal mining operations in 1913 amid the rush and optimism of the Progressive Era. Located in the Miami/Commerce district of Oklahoma, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. Rock from the lower levels ran so rich that hand-sorted high-grade ore was shipped directly to the smelter without milling. Smallpox swept through the miners' camp one winter, closing the boarding houses and slowing ore production for weeks. The mine's foreman kept meticulous logbooks that survive today, recording every shift's progress, accidents, and unusual ore encounters in careful copperplate. Two separate ore bodies were eventually found to merge at depth, proving the mine's long-term viability and unlocking a new phase of development. By the time operations wound down, the Miami Consolidated had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. Today the site is accessible via a Forest Service road and draws curious visitors who pick through the tailings for mineral samples.

locations.name: Miami Fraction
locations.description: The Miami Fraction was staked by experienced miners who recognized surface gossan typical of a buried sulfide body, launching zinc mining operations in 1905 amid the rush and optimism of the early twentieth century. Located in the Miami/Commerce district of Oklahoma, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. Drilling records fell repeatedly as miners pushed deeper, guided by candle-flame and the nose of experienced shift bosses. An early-morning powder explosion rocked the shaft house, shattering windows in the camp a quarter-mile away and briefly paralyzing operations. The company doctor, on call around the clock, stitched wounds and set bones in a crude surgery behind the shaft house. An aerial tramway carried buckets of ore from the portal to the mill, its cables and towers stretching dramatically across a quarter-mile of canyon. By the time operations wound down, the Miami Fraction had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. A nearby ghost town—just a scattering of cabin foundations and a collapsed false-front storefront—is a companion reminder of the mining era.

locations.name: Miami Star Lode
locations.description: The Miami Star Lode was staked when road-building crews blasted through previously unexplored terrain, launching base-metal mining operations in 1915 amid the rush and optimism of the Progressive Era. Located in the Miami/Commerce district of Oklahoma, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. The company sank a second shaft to ventilate the rapidly deepening stopes and allow faster removal of broken ore. The discovery of a rich new ore shoot in the lower levels reversed the mine's fortunes just as the owners were considering closure. Cornish hard-rock miners, known locally as 'Cousin Jacks,' dominated the skilled workforce and brought their traditional mining knowledge from Cornwall. Miles of lateral drifts radiated outward from the main shaft, following the irregular ore body through hard country rock. By the time operations wound down, the Miami Star Lode had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. An interpretive trail now winds through the mine yard, where plaques describe the daily life of miners who once filled these now-silent buildings.

locations.name: Fresno Mine
locations.description: The Fresno Mine was discovered after local ranchers brought samples to a territorial assay office, launching mercury mining operations in 1915 amid the rush and optimism of the Progressive Era. Located in the Terlingua district of Texas, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. Drilling records fell repeatedly as miners pushed deeper, guided by candle-flame and the nose of experienced shift bosses. An early-morning powder explosion rocked the shaft house, shattering windows in the camp a quarter-mile away and briefly paralyzing operations. The company doctor, on call around the clock, stitched wounds and set bones in a crude surgery behind the shaft house. An aerial tramway carried buckets of ore from the portal to the mill, its cables and towers stretching dramatically across a quarter-mile of canyon. By the time operations wound down, the Fresno Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. A nearby ghost town—just a scattering of cabin foundations and a collapsed false-front storefront—is a companion reminder of the mining era.

locations.name: Waldron Mine
locations.description: The Waldron Mine emerged when a territorial geologist reported promising mineral indications in his annual survey, launching mercury mining operations in 1901 amid the rush and optimism of the early twentieth century. Located in the Terlingua district of Texas, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. At its zenith the mine supported an entire community: assay office, machine shop, blacksmith, carpenter shop, and company store. The infamous hard-rock miners' strike of the era brought armed guards and Pinkerton detectives to the property, souring labor relations for years. Women ran boarding houses and laundries on the town's main street, forming an economic backbone that outlasted several booms and busts. The upper levels intersected a large underground lake that required constant pumping to keep the deep workings from flooding. By the time operations wound down, the Waldron Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The land has been partially reclaimed, but the scarred hillside and flat tailings terrace are unmistakable markers of the mine's industrial past.

locations.name: Terlingua Quicksilver Mine No.2
locations.description: The Terlingua Quicksilver Mine No.2 was located by methodical float sampling by a mining engineer working the district systematically, launching mercury mining operations in 1912 amid the rush and optimism of the Progressive Era. Located in the Terlingua district of Texas, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. During its busiest years the mine ran three full shifts, with a steady stream of ore wagons rumbling down the canyon road at all hours. A flash flood in the mill tailings pond in the late boom years forced a month-long shutdown and costly repairs to the crushing plant. The mine's Chinese workers, barred from the skilled drilling crews, excelled at the dangerous work of mucking and timbering the deeper levels. The ore body proved wider at depth than at the surface, encouraging ever-deeper sinking and driving the shaft to record levels. By the time operations wound down, the Terlingua Quicksilver Mine No.2 had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The mill foundations, settling ponds, and scattered machinery form an open-air industrial museum on BLM land near the old camp site.

locations.name: Big Bend Mercury Mine
locations.description: The Big Bend Mercury Mine was located by methodical float sampling by a mining engineer working the district systematically, launching mercury mining operations in 1912 amid the rush and optimism of the Progressive Era. Located in the Terlingua district of Texas, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. The company imported the latest Cornish pump technology to battle the underground water that threatened to drown the deeper workings. Persistent underground flooding in the lower levels prompted the costly installation of a Cornish pump that became the largest in the district. A charismatic labor organizer arrived one summer and spent months quietly signing up men before calling a strike that shut the mine for weeks. The mill sat in the valley below, its stamp batteries rattling day and night and its tailings pond spreading across the old floodplain. By the time operations wound down, the Big Bend Mercury Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. Rockhounds visit seasonally, working through the tailings for fragments of the original ore: chalcopyrite, pyrite, quartz crystals, and native metal.

locations.name: Mariscal Mine
locations.description: The Mariscal Mine was discovered after local ranchers brought samples to a territorial assay office, launching mercury mining operations in 1906 amid the rush and optimism of the early twentieth century. Located in the Terlingua district of Texas, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. Management secured contracts to supply a regional smelter, locking in favorable prices and driving expansion of the underground workings. A price collapse on world markets forced a temporary shutdown that lasted two years, scattering the workforce across the region. Seasoned veterans who had worked every major camp in the West ended up here, drawn by the reputation of the ore body. Miners sank the main shaft to depths exceeding a thousand feet, honeycombing the mountain with stopes and connecting drifts. By the time operations wound down, the Mariscal Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The headframe and ore bins remain standing, weather-beaten but intact, managed by a local historical society that leads occasional tours.

locations.name: Terlingua Star Lode
locations.description: The Terlingua Star Lode came to attention after a geological survey noted the unusual mineralization in the district, launching mercury mining operations in 1887 amid the rush and optimism of the Gilded Age. Located in the Terlingua district of Texas, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. The company sank a second shaft to ventilate the rapidly deepening stopes and allow faster removal of broken ore. The discovery of a rich new ore shoot in the lower levels reversed the mine's fortunes just as the owners were considering closure. Cornish hard-rock miners, known locally as 'Cousin Jacks,' dominated the skilled workforce and brought their traditional mining knowledge from Cornwall. Miles of lateral drifts radiated outward from the main shaft, following the irregular ore body through hard country rock. By the time operations wound down, the Terlingua Star Lode had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. An interpretive trail now winds through the mine yard, where plaques describe the daily life of miners who once filled these now-silent buildings.

locations.name: Terlingua Chief
locations.description: The Terlingua Chief was identified when a prospector's assay of odd-colored rock returned unexpectedly high values, launching mercury mining operations in 1912 amid the rush and optimism of the Progressive Era. Located in the Terlingua district of Texas, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. In its prime the property was valued in the millions and attracted investors from as far as London and New York. A fire in the timber sets of the upper level destroyed several hundred feet of drift and set production back by an entire season. The mine superintendent was a hard-driving Scotsman famous for pushing men and machines beyond their rated limits. A decline adit near the base of the ridge drained the lower workings and doubled as a haulage way for loaded ore cars. By the time operations wound down, the Terlingua Chief had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. Little remains above ground beyond the concrete foundations of the shaft house and the long gray ribbon of tailings across the hillside.

locations.name: Terlingua Pride
locations.description: The Terlingua Pride was found when surface trenching exposed a rich vein of metallic ore, launching mercury mining operations in 1907 amid the rush and optimism of the early twentieth century. Located in the Terlingua district of Texas, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. The operation employed hundreds of men at its height, filling a nearby camp with boarding houses, saloons, and a school for miners' children. A runaway ore skip in the main shaft killed two men and injured four others, prompting the installation of new safety catches on all hoists. The owner, a self-made man who had arrived with nothing but a grubstake and an iron will, became one of the wealthiest citizens in the territory. The main shaft was timbered with massive square sets, each joint hand-fitted by skilled carpenters, creating a cathedral-like underground gallery. By the time operations wound down, the Terlingua Pride had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The shaft is capped and fenced for safety, but hikers can still trace the ore-cart rails to the edge of the old mill site.

The main shaft was timbered with massive square sets, each joint hand-fitted by skilled carpenters, creating a cathedral-like underground gallery. By the time operations wound down, the Tres Hermanas Mine (TX) had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The shaft is capped and fenced for safety, but hikers can still trace the ore-cart rails to the edge of the old mill site.

locations.name: Capote Mine
locations.description: The Capote Mine emerged when a freighter's mule kicked loose a chunk of ore that assayed three hundred ounces silver per ton, launching silver mining operations in 1903 amid the rush and optimism of the early twentieth century. Located in the Shafter district of Texas, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. In its prime the property was valued in the millions and attracted investors from as far as London and New York. A fire in the timber sets of the upper level destroyed several hundred feet of drift and set production back by an entire season. The mine superintendent was a hard-driving Scotsman famous for pushing men and machines beyond their rated limits. A decline adit near the base of the ridge drained the lower workings and doubled as a haulage way for loaded ore cars. By the time operations wound down, the Capote Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. Little remains above ground beyond the concrete foundations of the shaft house and the long gray ribbon of tailings across the hillside.

locations.description: The Pinto Canyon Mine came to light after a storm washed away hillside debris and exposed a wide streak of galena, launching silver mining operations in 1888 amid the rush and optimism of the Gilded Age. locations.name: Alamito Creek Mine
locations.description: The Alamito Creek Mine came to light after a storm washed away hillside debris and exposed a wide streak of galena, launching silver mining operations in 1899 amid the rush and optimism of the final frontier boom. Located in the Shafter district of Texas, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. During its busiest years the mine ran three full shifts, with a steady stream of ore wagons rumbling down the canyon road at all hours. A flash flood in the mill tailings pond in the late boom years forced a month-long shutdown and costly repairs to the crushing plant. The mine's Chinese workers, barred from the skilled drilling crews, excelled at the dangerous work of mucking and timbering the deeper levels. The ore body proved wider at depth than at the surface, encouraging ever-deeper sinking and driving the shaft to record levels. By the time operations wound down, the Alamito Creek Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The mill foundations, settling ponds, and scattered machinery form an open-air industrial museum on BLM land near the old camp site.

locations.name: Shafter Champion
locations.description: The Shafter Champion was discovered by Mexican prospectors following a century-old Spanish trail to a recorded silver source, launching silver mining operations in 1910 amid the rush and optimism of the Progressive Era. Located in the Shafter district of Texas, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. An on-site reduction plant was erected to process lower-grade material, squeezing every dollar of value from the ore body. A catastrophic cave-in in one of the peak years trapped a dozen men underground for three days before a rescue drift broke through. The workforce was a polyglot mix of Irish, Welsh, Scandinavian, and Chinese laborers, each group occupying its own corner of the sprawling camp. Underground temperatures climbed dangerously in the deeper workings, forcing the company to improve ventilation and supply ice water to drilling crews. By the time operations wound down, the Shafter Champion had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. Bureau of Land Management maintains public access to the surface workings; the main shaft is gated but visible from the edge of the collar.

locations.name: Llano Iron Mine
locations.description: The Llano Iron Mine was uncovered when a road-grading crew blasted through a hillside and hit a lens of rich cerargyrite, launching silver mining operations in 1881 amid the rush and optimism of the Gilded Age. Located in the Llano district of Texas, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. Output climbed year after year through the boom decade, funding fine homes, a brick bank, and a narrow-gauge railroad spur to the mill. A dynamite misfire in the deepest stope caused a partial collapse that required months of careful timbering before work could resume. Families settled nearby, filling a small town with a church, a one-room school, and a lively Independence Day celebration that drew miners from miles around. The surface plant grew over the decades to include a large headframe, ore bins, a rock crusher, a flotation mill, and a leaching yard. By the time operations wound down, the Llano Iron Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The site is listed on the National Register of Historic Places, ensuring basic preservation of the surviving surface features.

locations.description: The Enchanted Rock Mine emerged from the search of hardened veterans who had already worked out a dozen smaller claims, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1880 amid the rush and optimism of the Gilded Age. locations.name: Packsaddle Mine
locations.description: The Packsaddle Mine was discovered by a group of forty-niners following the classic miner's instinct—pick up every likely rock, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1887 amid the rush and optimism of the Gilded Age. Located in the Llano district of Texas, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. Drilling records fell repeatedly as miners pushed deeper, guided by candle-flame and the nose of experienced shift bosses. An early-morning powder explosion rocked the shaft house, shattering windows in the camp a quarter-mile away and briefly paralyzing operations. The company doctor, on call around the clock, stitched wounds and set bones in a crude surgery behind the shaft house. An aerial tramway carried buckets of ore from the portal to the mill, its cables and towers stretching dramatically across a quarter-mile of canyon. By the time operations wound down, the Packsaddle Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. A nearby ghost town—just a scattering of cabin foundations and a collapsed false-front storefront—is a companion reminder of the mining era.

locations.name: Valley Spring Mine
locations.description: The Valley Spring Mine emerged when a territorial geologist reported promising mineral indications in his annual survey, launching iron mining operations in 1910 amid the rush and optimism of the Progressive Era. Located in the Llano district of Texas, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. At peak output the headframe ran day and night; the stamp mill's rhythmic thunder could be heard ten miles away across the valley. Labor unrest in the early twentieth century brought in the Western Federation of Miners, and a bitter strike closed the mine for nearly a year. A tight-knit community of Slavic immigrants, many recruited directly from Bohemian and Croatian mining villages, formed the backbone of the labor force. Three separate shafts served different levels, connected by a network of raises and manways that allowed workers to move through the mine safely. By the time operations wound down, the Valley Spring Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. A state historic park now preserves the surface structures, operating a small visitor center and offering summer tours of the mine yard.
