locations.name: Llano Star Lode
locations.description: The Llano Star Lode emerged from careful float sampling by a Denver assayer who spent two seasons tracing the vein uphill, launching silver mining operations in 1886 amid the rush and optimism of the Gilded Age. Located in the Llano district of Texas, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. Management secured contracts to supply a regional smelter, locking in favorable prices and driving expansion of the underground workings. A price collapse on world markets forced a temporary shutdown that lasted two years, scattering the workforce across the region. Seasoned veterans who had worked every major camp in the West ended up here, drawn by the reputation of the ore body. Miners sank the main shaft to depths exceeding a thousand feet, honeycombing the mountain with stopes and connecting drifts. By the time operations wound down, the Llano Star Lode had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The headframe and ore bins remain standing, weather-beaten but intact, managed by a local historical society that leads occasional tours.

locations.name: Llano Consolidated
locations.description: The Llano Consolidated was staked by a greenhorn who mistook the heavy gray ore for lead—until the assay came back, launching silver mining operations in 1878 amid the rush and optimism of the post-war silver boom. Located in the Llano district of Texas, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. The company imported the latest Cornish pump technology to battle the underground water that threatened to drown the deeper workings. Persistent underground flooding in the lower levels prompted the costly installation of a Cornish pump that became the largest in the district. A charismatic labor organizer arrived one summer and spent months quietly signing up men before calling a strike that shut the mine for weeks. The mill sat in the valley below, its stamp batteries rattling day and night and its tailings pond spreading across the old floodplain. By the time operations wound down, the Llano Consolidated had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. Rockhounds visit seasonally, working through the tailings for fragments of the original ore: chalcopyrite, pyrite, quartz crystals, and native metal.

locations.name: Black Hills Mine No.103
locations.description: The Black Hills Mine No.103 was identified by a government geologist who recognized the telltale iron gossan capping a rich lode, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1886 amid the rush and optimism of the Gilded Age. Located in the Lead/Homestake district of South Dakota, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. Rock from the lower levels ran so rich that hand-sorted high-grade ore was shipped directly to the smelter without milling. Smallpox swept through the miners' camp one winter, closing the boarding houses and slowing ore production for weeks. The mine's foreman kept meticulous logbooks that survive today, recording every shift's progress, accidents, and unusual ore encounters in careful copperplate. Two separate ore bodies were eventually found to merge at depth, proving the mine's long-term viability and unlocking a new phase of development. By the time operations wound down, the Black Hills Mine No.103 had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. Today the site is accessible via a Forest Service road and draws curious visitors who pick through the tailings for mineral samples.

The infamous hard-rock miners' strike of the era brought armed guards and Pinkerton detectives to the property, souring labor relations for years. By the time operations wound down, the Black Hills Mine No.108 had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The land has been partially reclaimed, but the scarred hillside and flat tailings terrace are unmistakable markers of the mine's industrial past.

locations.name: Black Hills Mine No.113
locations.description: The Black Hills Mine No.113 was staked after local ranchers found suspicious yellow glints in a dry wash near their pasture, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1908 amid the rush and optimism of the early twentieth century. Located in the Lead/Homestake district of South Dakota, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. Output climbed year after year through the boom decade, funding fine homes, a brick bank, and a narrow-gauge railroad spur to the mill. A dynamite misfire in the deepest stope caused a partial collapse that required months of careful timbering before work could resume. Families settled nearby, filling a small town with a church, a one-room school, and a lively Independence Day celebration that drew miners from miles around. The surface plant grew over the decades to include a large headframe, ore bins, a rock crusher, a flotation mill, and a leaching yard. By the time operations wound down, the Black Hills Mine No.113 had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The site is listed on the National Register of Historic Places, ensuring basic preservation of the surviving surface features.

locations.name: Black Hills Mine No.114
locations.description: The Black Hills Mine No.114 came to notice when a surveying party camped on the hillside and examined the local float rock, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1907 amid the rush and optimism of the early twentieth century. Located in the Lead/Homestake district of South Dakota, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. Drilling records fell repeatedly as miners pushed deeper, guided by candle-flame and the nose of experienced shift bosses. An early-morning powder explosion rocked the shaft house, shattering windows in the camp a quarter-mile away and briefly paralyzing operations. The company doctor, on call around the clock, stitched wounds and set bones in a crude surgery behind the shaft house. An aerial tramway carried buckets of ore from the portal to the mill, its cables and towers stretching dramatically across a quarter-mile of canyon. By the time operations wound down, the Black Hills Mine No.114 had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. A nearby ghost town—just a scattering of cabin foundations and a collapsed false-front storefront—is a companion reminder of the mining era.

locations.name: Black Hills Mine No.115
locations.description: The Black Hills Mine No.115 was uncovered by a former sea captain who had traded his ship's compass for a miner's pick, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1908 amid the rush and optimism of the early twentieth century. Located in the Lead/Homestake district of South Dakota, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. At peak output the headframe ran day and night; the stamp mill's rhythmic thunder could be heard ten miles away across the valley. Labor unrest in the early twentieth century brought in the Western Federation of Miners, and a bitter strike closed the mine for nearly a year. A tight-knit community of Slavic immigrants, many recruited directly from Bohemian and Croatian mining villages, formed the backbone of the labor force. Three separate shafts served different levels, connected by a network of raises and manways that allowed workers to move through the mine safely. By the time operations wound down, the Black Hills Mine No.115 had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. A state historic park now preserves the surface structures, operating a small visitor center and offering summer tours of the mine yard.

locations.name: Black Hills Mine No.116
locations.description: The Black Hills Mine No.116 was staked after local ranchers found suspicious yellow glints in a dry wash near their pasture, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1913 amid the rush and optimism of the Progressive Era. Located in the Lead/Homestake district of South Dakota, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. During its busiest years the mine ran three full shifts, with a steady stream of ore wagons rumbling down the canyon road at all hours. A flash flood in the mill tailings pond in the late boom years forced a month-long shutdown and costly repairs to the crushing plant. The mine's Chinese workers, barred from the skilled drilling crews, excelled at the dangerous work of mucking and timbering the deeper levels. The ore body proved wider at depth than at the surface, encouraging ever-deeper sinking and driving the shaft to record levels. By the time operations wound down, the Black Hills Mine No.116 had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The mill foundations, settling ponds, and scattered machinery form an open-air industrial museum on BLM land near the old camp site.

locations.name: Black Hills Mine No.117
locations.description: The Black Hills Mine No.117 came to notice when a surveying party camped on the hillside and examined the local float rock, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1907 amid the rush and optimism of the early twentieth century. Located in the Lead/Homestake district of South Dakota, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. At its zenith the mine supported an entire community: assay office, machine shop, blacksmith, carpenter shop, and company store. The infamous hard-rock miners' strike of the era brought armed guards and Pinkerton detectives to the property, souring labor relations for years. Women ran boarding houses and laundries on the town's main street, forming an economic backbone that outlasted several booms and busts. The upper levels intersected a large underground lake that required constant pumping to keep the deep workings from flooding. By the time operations wound down, the Black Hills Mine No.117 had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The land has been partially reclaimed, but the scarred hillside and flat tailings terrace are unmistakable markers of the mine's industrial past.

locations.name: Black Hills Mine No.118
locations.description: The Black Hills Mine No.118 emerged from the search of hardened veterans who had already worked out a dozen smaller claims, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1906 amid the rush and optimism of the early twentieth century. Located in the Lead/Homestake district of South Dakota, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. In its prime the property was valued in the millions and attracted investors from as far as London and New York. A fire in the timber sets of the upper level destroyed several hundred feet of drift and set production back by an entire season. The mine superintendent was a hard-driving Scotsman famous for pushing men and machines beyond their rated limits. A decline adit near the base of the ridge drained the lower workings and doubled as a haulage way for loaded ore cars. By the time operations wound down, the Black Hills Mine No.118 had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. Little remains above ground beyond the concrete foundations of the shaft house and the long gray ribbon of tailings across the hillside.

Located in the Lead/Homestake district of South Dakota, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. At its zenith the mine supported an entire community: assay office, machine shop, blacksmith, carpenter shop, and company store. The infamous hard-rock miners' strike of the era brought armed guards and Pinkerton detectives to the property, souring labor relations for years. Women ran boarding houses and laundries on the town's main street, forming an economic backbone that outlasted several booms and busts. The upper levels intersected a large underground lake that required constant pumping to keep the deep workings from flooding. By the time operations wound down, the Black Hills Mine No.119 had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The land has been partially reclaimed, but the scarred hillside and flat tailings terrace are unmistakable markers of the mine's industrial past.
